proposals.title: Permutation Properties of Polynomials with Coefficients in Extension Fields
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation polynomials over finite fields is central to cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. Recent work, including Ding's own, has focused on polynomials with coefficients in the base field 𝔽_q. However, many constructions in these applications naturally involve elements from extension fields. A systematic study of permutation behavior when coefficients are drawn from 𝔽_{q^m} is lacking and could unify and generalize known sporadic results.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by allowing coefficients from a finite extension field 𝔽_{q^m}, new infinite families of permutation polynomials over 𝔽_{q^n} can be discovered, and that known permutation criteria (like the AGW criterion and Hermite's criterion) can be effectively generalized to this broader setting.
proposals.method: We will focus on specific low-degree forms, such as linearized polynomials, binomials, and trinomials, where coefficients are in 𝔽_{q^m} and the variable is in 𝔽_{q^n}. The core method involves using the trace and norm functions from 𝔽_{q^n} down to subfields to analyze the mapping's injectivity. We will extend the 'subfield decomposition' technique, treating the polynomial as a sum of component maps over subfields, and apply character sum arguments to establish permutation conditions. The analysis will be specialized to cases where m divides n, exploiting the field lattice.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for constructing permutation polynomials, potentially yielding new infinite classes. It will extend the theoretical understanding of permutation criteria to the extension field coefficient case, offering a more powerful toolkit for designing cryptographic S-boxes and complete mappings with desired algebraic properties.
proposals.novelty: While permutation polynomials with base-field coefficients are well-studied, the explicit and systematic investigation of polynomials whose coefficients are *required* to be in a proper extension field is novel. This moves beyond the typical ad-hoc examples and aims for a general theory.

proposals.title: Inverting the Permutation Condition: Characterizing Finite Fields for Exceptional Permutation Polynomials with Prescribed Coefficients
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation polynomials (PPs) over finite fields is central to cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. While most research focuses on proving a given polynomial is a PP over a specific field, the inverse problem—determining for which fields a polynomial with fixed coefficients permutes—is less explored but crucial for constructing flexible cryptographic primitives and understanding the deep arithmetic of exceptional polynomials. This directly extends recent work on complete mappings and permutation pentanomials.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given parametric family of polynomials (e.g., the pentanomial or trinomial forms from recent papers), the set of finite fields over which it is a permutation polynomial can be characterized by a finite set of arithmetic conditions on the field's order and its residue modulo a specific integer, governed by the polynomial's Galois group and resultant calculations.
proposals.method: We will fix a parametric form, such as F(X)=aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3 over 𝔽_{q^2}, with parameters (a,b,c,d) in a base field. Using the multivariate resultant and elimination theory, we will derive the condition for F(X)-F(Y) to have no zeroes off the diagonal. This translates to the non-vanishing of a discriminant as a rational function in the field size q. We will then use the theory of exceptional polynomials, linking permutation properties to the polynomial's indecomposability and monodromy groups, to determine the infinite set of extension fields where these conditions hold.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, systematic framework for the 'inverse' classification of permutation polynomials, moving from parameter searches over one field to field characterizations for fixed parameters. It will yield concrete, computable criteria for field sizes, directly applicable to the design of cryptographic S-boxes with desired field agility. The results will also provide new insights into the Carlitz-Wan conjecture on exceptional polynomials by examining its converse for specific forms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically inverting the classical permutation polynomial problem—shifting the object of classification from the polynomial to the field—and applying advanced tools from algebraic geometry (resultants, Galois theory of function fields) to a concrete problem in finite fields, a synthesis not prominently featured in pre-2026 PP literature.

proposals.title: Characterizing Permutation Polynomials via Weil Sums: A Unified Approach for Multinomials and Exceptional Cases
proposals.motivation: Permutation polynomials over finite fields are fundamental in cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. While many specific families have been constructed and tested, a unified theoretical framework for proving permutation properties, especially for complex multinomials and exceptional polynomials, remains a significant challenge. Character sums, particularly Weil sums, offer a powerful but underexploited tool for deriving necessary and sufficient conditions beyond Hermite's criterion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic application of Weil sum bounds and character sum identities can yield explicit, testable algebraic criteria that characterize permutation behavior for broad classes of polynomials, including the pentanomials and complete mappings studied in recent work, and can be extended to investigate exceptionality.
proposals.method: The core method is to express the permutation condition (F(x)-F(y) has no roots for x≠y) in terms of character sums over the multiplicative and additive groups of the finite field. We will compute and bound Weil sums associated with the polynomial F(X) and its difference polynomials F(X+a)-F(X). Specifically, we will apply the classical Weil bound for multiplicative character sums and the more recent bounds for mixed character sums (e.g., Katz, Wan) to derive inequalities that force the permutation property only when certain coefficient equations hold. This will involve symbolic computation in SageMath or Magma to handle the algebra of specific polynomial forms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, character-sum-based toolkit for proving and classifying permutation polynomials, moving beyond case-by-case verification. It will likely produce new infinite families of permutation multinomials and contribute to the classification of exceptional permutation polynomials, offering deeper insight into the Carlitz-Wan conjecture on exceptionality. The results will be concrete theorems with verifiable parameter conditions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted synthesis of Weil sum techniques, typically used for proving randomness or bounding solutions, with the explicit algebraic characterization problem for permutation polynomials. While character sums are known in theory, their systematic application to derive necessary coefficient conditions for the specific multinomial and exceptional forms in Ding's recent papers is a novel and non-obvious direction.

proposals.title: Permutation Polynomials over Finite Fields with Exponent Q Not a Power of the Characteristic
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation polynomials (PPs) of the form X^r h(X^(Q-1)) over F_{q^n}, where Q is a power of the field characteristic p, is a well-established framework, leveraging the multiplicative group structure of F_{q^n}^*. This condition is crucial for applying Hermite's criterion via character sums and the Carlitz-Wan theory. However, it remains unclear whether the permutation property can be systematically characterized when Q is not a p-power, a scenario that disrupts the standard additive and multiplicative structures used in existing proofs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for certain structured subclasses of rational functions or sparse polynomials, the permutation property over F_{q^n} can be determined even when the exponent Q is coprime to p, leading to new infinite families of PPs and a deeper understanding of the interplay between additive and multiplicative orders in finite fields.
proposals.method: We will first analyze specific forms from recent work, such as aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3, but treat the exponent 'q' as a parameter Q not necessarily a p-power. The core approach involves adapting the multivariate resultant and factorization methods used in proving the Carlitz-Wan conjecture for exceptional polynomials. We will employ character sum evaluations over subgroups of F_{q^n}^* of order (Q-1) and develop new criteria by examining the factorization of associated equations over field extensions, using tools from the theory of function field extensions and Galois theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the theoretical framework for constructing and classifying permutation polynomials, moving beyond the characteristic-power constraint. It will provide new infinite families of PPs and potentially yield counterexamples or conditions showing when the classical framework fails. The results will contribute to applications in cryptography and coding theory where PPs with non-standard exponents are of interest.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically relaxing the fundamental assumption that Q is a p-power, which underpins nearly all prior analytic methods for this polynomial form. Investigating this uncharted parameter regime requires novel combinations of algebraic and combinatorial arguments distinct from the standard character sum techniques.

proposals.title: p-adic Lifting and Congruence Criteria for Exceptional Permutation Polynomials over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: The classification of permutation polynomials (PPs) and complete mappings over finite fields of characteristic p is a central problem in finite field theory with applications in cryptography and coding. Direct combinatorial and algebraic methods for verifying permutation properties, especially for high-degree or sparse polynomials like pentanomials, become computationally intractable. A new analytic framework is needed to derive more efficient, characteristic-specific criteria.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that p-adic analytic methods, specifically techniques of lifting polynomials to characteristic zero and analyzing p-adic Newton polygons and associated L-functions, can yield powerful new congruence conditions that characterize exceptional permutation polynomials and complete mappings in characteristic p.
proposals.method: We will lift polynomials over F_{p^n} to polynomials over p-adic fields (e.g., unramified extensions of Q_p). The core method involves studying the p-adic Newton polygon of the lifted polynomial and its associated L-function to derive p-adic congruences that are necessary and sufficient for the original polynomial to be a permutation. This will be applied to structured classes, such as sparse polynomials of the form aX^{kq+l} + ... and extensions of local fields, building on the Carlitz-Wan conjecture framework. The analysis will focus on the p-adic valuation of coefficients and resultant-like invariants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new p-adic toolkit for the study of permutation polynomials, leading to novel, theoretically grounded criteria for verifying permutation properties. It will potentially resolve open cases in the classification of sparse PPs (e.g., certain pentanomials) and offer a deeper understanding of the 'exceptional' property via analytic continuation from characteristic zero.
proposals.novelty: While p-adic analysis is classical, its systematic application to the explicit construction and classification of permutation polynomials and complete mappings in characteristic p is novel. It bridges the analytic number theory of p-adic L-functions with the combinatorial algebra of finite fields in a non-obvious way.

proposals.title: On the Value Set Sizes of Complete Mapping Polynomials and Their Variants over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: Research on permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields has been extensive, with a strong focus on characterizing when a polynomial is a permutation. However, for the non-permutation cases of these structured polynomials, the distribution and size of their value sets are poorly understood. Determining these value set sizes is a fundamental combinatorial problem with applications in coding theory, cryptography, and the design of sequences, providing a natural dual to the permutation problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for the specific classes of polynomials studied in recent permutation/complete mapping papers (e.g., pentanomials and trinomials of specific forms), the size of their value sets in the non-permutation case can be explicitly bounded or even determined exactly using algebraic and combinatorial methods related to their associated rational functions and curve equations.
proposals.method: We will analyze polynomials of the form f(X)=aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3 and related pentanomials over F_{q^2}. The core method involves studying the equation f(X)=f(Y) to count distinct values, linking it to the rationality of the associated curve (f(X)-f(Y))/(X-Y)=0. We will employ techniques from the theory of function fields, Weil bounds, and the analysis of exceptional polynomials to bound the number of F_q-rational points on these curves, thereby bounding the value set size V_f. For specific parameter sets, we aim to derive exact formulas by determining when the associated rational function is indecomposable and studying its ramification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic bounds and exact formulas for the value set sizes of these important polynomial classes outside the permutation case. It will establish a new direction linking the study of complete mappings and permutation polynomials to the classical value set problem, enriching both areas. The results will offer new tools for constructing functions with prescribed image sizes for applications.
proposals.novelty: While the permutation property of these polynomials has been determined, their non-injective behavior has not been quantified. This proposal shifts the focus from a binary (permutation/non-permutation) classification to a quantitative measure of non-injectivity, applying the deep algebraic structure already uncovered for permutations to solve a distinct, hard combinatorial problem.

proposals.title: Infinite Sequences of Exceptional Polynomials and Complete Mappings over Fields of Characteristic Three
proposals.motivation: The construction of infinite sequences of exceptional polynomials over finite fields, particularly those yielding permutation polynomials and complete mappings, is a central problem in finite field arithmetic with applications in cryptography and combinatorial designs. Prior work, including the Carlitz-Wan conjecture and its extensions, has largely focused on odd characteristics (especially p>3), leaving characteristic 3 as a significant gap due to distinct algebraic obstructions like the failure of the derivative test for permutation behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the theoretical framework for constructing infinite sequences of exceptional polynomials, particularly those of the form involving sparse multivariate expressions like aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3, can be systematically adapted to characteristic 3, leading to new infinite families of permutation polynomials and complete mappings.
proposals.method: We will first analyze the arithmetic and Galois-theoretic properties specific to extensions of local fields in characteristic 3. Using the connection between exceptional polynomials and ramification theory, we will adapt the 'lifting' constructions from characteristic p>3. The core technical step will involve a detailed case-by-case study of rational functions and their associated polynomials over F_{3^n}, modifying the exponent conditions and coefficient constraints from known sequences to avoid the pitfalls of inseparable derivatives. Computational verification for small fields using SageMath will guide the theoretical generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic library of infinite exceptional polynomial sequences in characteristic 3, explicitly solving open cases for permutation pentanomials and complete mappings. It will extend the validity of the Carlitz-Wan-type conjectures to p=3, enriching the classification theory of permutation polynomials.
proposals.novelty: The extension to characteristic 3 is non-trivial because standard techniques leveraging non-vanishing derivatives fail; the novelty lies in developing new algebraic criteria for permutation behavior in this setting and adapting the infinite sequence construction machinery, which has been largely confined to odd p>3.

proposals.title: Monodromy Groups of Exceptional Polynomials and Complete Permutations over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: In arithmetic statistics and finite field theory, exceptional polynomials and complete mappings are central to constructing objects like difference sets and permutation polynomials. The Carlitz-Wan conjecture, proven by Fried et al., links exceptionality to non-solvable monodromy groups, but explicit determination of these groups for specific, newly discovered polynomial families remains an open and computationally intensive problem. A detailed study of these groups deepens our understanding of the fundamental Galois-theoretic structure underpinning exceptionality and permutation properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the monodromy groups of newly discovered classes of permutation pentanomials and complete mappings of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3 are classical linear groups (e.g., PSL(2, q') or symmetric/alternating groups) in their natural representations, and that their structure directly explains and classifies the exceptional nature and permutation behavior of these functions.
proposals.method: We will compute the monodromy groups (arithmetic and geometric) by analyzing the Galois groups of the function field extensions F_q(t) → F_q(x) defined by f(X) - t = 0, where f is a target polynomial. The method combines symbolic computation in Magma or Sage (for polynomial factorization, resolvent construction, and group identification) with theoretical techniques from Fried's rigidity and lifting criteria. For the parametric families, we will determine the generic monodromy group over Q and then specialize to finite fields, applying the theory of exceptional covers to handle ramification and inertia groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit monodromy group classifications for several recently discovered infinite families of permutation polynomials, offering a unified Galois-theoretic explanation for their properties. It will also produce new examples of rigid tuples in classical groups and potentially identify new exceptional covers, enriching the database of known connections between group theory and polynomial dynamics over finite fields.
proposals.novelty: While monodromy groups of exceptional polynomials are known to be non-solvable in general, the precise group structure for the specific, multi-parameter families arising from recent work on permutation pentanomials and complete mappings has not been determined. The novelty lies in applying and extending the computational Galois theory toolkit to these concrete, complex families to extract their underlying group-theoretic uniformity.

proposals.title: Constructing Exceptional Functions with Prescribed Non-Solvable Monodromy Groups over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: The classification of exceptional functions, which are permutation polynomials over infinitely many finite fields, is a central problem in arithmetic dynamics and finite geometry. While the forward problem—determining the monodromy group of a given function—is well-studied, the inverse problem of constructing a function from a prescribed group remains largely open, especially for non-solvable groups. Solving this inverse problem would provide a systematic method for generating new families of exceptional functions with desired symmetry properties.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific families of non-solvable permutation groups (e.g., PSL(2, q) or alternating groups A_n, n≥5), one can explicitly construct rational functions or sparse polynomials over global fields whose reductions yield exceptional functions realizing these groups as geometric monodromy groups over finite fields.
proposals.method: We will leverage the known classification of indecomposable exceptional functions and their relation to finite simple groups. The approach will combine: 1) Constructing covers of the projective line over number fields with a specified monodromy group using rigidity and rationality criteria. 2) Applying the Carlitz-Wan conjecture (now a theorem, pre-2026) and its generalizations to ensure the exceptional property for reductions. 3) Specializing the construction to produce explicit sparse polynomial forms, such as linearized sums of few monomials (e.g., pentanomials of specific shapes over F_{q^2}), guided by the researcher's recent work on permutation pentanomials and complete mappings.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first explicit families of exceptional functions with prescribed non-solvable monodromy, advancing the inverse Galois problem for function fields over finite fields. It will also yield new, computationally efficient permutation polynomials for cryptographic applications, such as S-box design, with provably high algebraic complexity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reversing the classical direction of analysis—from group to function—for the critical non-solvable case, and in synthesizing monodromy techniques from inverse Galois theory with the arithmetic of exceptional polynomials from finite field theory.

proposals.title: Permutation Polynomials and Complete Mappings over Finite Rings of Composite Order
proposals.motivation: The theory of permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields 𝔽_q is well-developed, with applications in cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. However, many real-world algebraic structures, such as residue class rings ℤ_n, are not fields. A systematic study of these functions over general finite rings, where zero divisors exist, is largely unexplored and would extend the applicability of this algebraic theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging the structure of finite rings via the Chinese Remainder Theorem and local methods, one can classify and construct permutation polynomials and complete mappings over rings like ℤ_{p^e} and direct products, leading to new exceptional functions not seen in the field case.
proposals.method: We will generalize the study of polynomials of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3 and permutation pentanomials from 𝔽_{q^2} to rings ℤ_{p^e} and ℤ_n. The core approach is to decompose the problem via the CRT into studying functions over local rings ℤ_{p^e}, where we will employ Hensel lifting techniques and analyze differential criteria for permutation behavior in the presence of nilpotent elements. We will develop algorithms to test and generate permutation polynomials by checking conditions modulo prime powers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for permutation polynomials over finite rings, producing new infinite families of such functions. It will directly extend results like the Carlitz-Wan conjecture on exceptional polynomials to a broader algebraic context, potentially revealing new connections with modular arithmetic and integer-valued polynomials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond the prime-power field paradigm to the structurally richer and more complex setting of finite rings with zero divisors, a transition that is non-trivial and requires new theoretical tools distinct from classical finite field theory.

proposals.title: Exceptional Polynomials and Complete Mappings over Fields of Characteristic Zero
proposals.motivation: The theory of permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields is well-developed, with deep results on exceptional polynomials and their connections to the Carlitz-Wan conjecture. However, their systematic study over fields of characteristic zero, such as the rationals or reals, remains largely unexplored. Extending these investigations can unify finite field combinatorics with classical arithmetic dynamics and reveal new structural properties of polynomial maps.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the exceptionality and permutation properties of polynomial classes studied over finite fields (e.g., permutation pentanomials and complete mappings of specific forms) can be meaningfully defined and characterized over characteristic zero fields, leading to new infinite families of exceptional rational functions and a transfer principle linking local and global behavior.
proposals.method: We will adapt the definition of exceptionality to number fields, requiring the polynomial to be a permutation map modulo infinitely many primes. The core method involves analyzing specific forms, such as aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3, by treating q as a formal exponent and studying the associated algebraic curves and Galois groups over ℚ. We will employ tools from arithmetic geometry, including the study of functional decomposition and the construction of rational maps with prescribed reduction properties, to classify complete mappings over ℝ and ℚ.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for exceptional polynomials in characteristic zero, producing the first systematic classification of complete polynomial mappings over the rationals and reals. It will bridge finite field combinatorics with arithmetic dynamics, potentially yielding new insights into the global-to-local principles in number theory.
proposals.novelty: While exceptional polynomials are classically defined relative to finite fields, defining and studying their direct analogues over characteristic zero fields is a novel direction. The approach of transplanting specific, hard-won finite field constructions (like permutation pentanomials) into this new setting is non-obvious and unexplored.

proposals.title: Conjugacy Classes of Rational Maps over Finite Fields: Generalizing Permutation and Exceptional Functions
proposals.motivation: In finite fields, understanding the structure of permutation polynomials and exceptional polynomials is crucial for applications in cryptography and coding theory. Recent work has introduced new families of rational maps (e.g., complete mappings of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3), but their relationship to classical families via conjugacy—a powerful equivalence relation preserving dynamical and permutation properties—remains largely unexplored. Systematically studying these conjugacies can unify disparate constructions and reveal new classes of functions with desirable properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that several newly proposed rational maps, particularly those involving linearized or multivariate monomial compositions, are conjugate via fractional linear transformations (Möbius transformations) to known families of exceptional or permutation polynomials, and that this conjugacy framework can generate infinite new families of such functions.
proposals.method: We will first catalog recent rational map families from permutation and complete mapping literature (e.g., pentanomials, multinomials of shape X^{aq+b}). For each family, we will algorithmically search for conjugacy by solving, over appropriate extensions, the functional equation φ∘f∘φ^{-1} = g, where φ is a Möbius transformation over the algebraic closure, f is the new map, and g is a classical polynomial (e.g., monomials, Dickson polynomials). This involves symbolic computation with resultants and Gröbner bases to determine existence and parameter constraints. The classification will be extended using tools from the theory of exceptional covers and the Carlitz-Wan conjecture methodology.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a systematic classification of rational maps via conjugacy, leading to new infinite families of permutation and exceptional functions. It will provide a unifying algebraic framework that explains the existence of sporadic constructions and may simplify proofs of permutation properties by reduction to known cases.
proposals.novelty: While conjugacy is known for polynomials, its systematic application to the broader class of rational maps, especially those recently proposed in complete mapping and permutation contexts, is novel. The approach connects the study of specific multinomial forms to the deep theory of exceptional covers and dynamical equivalence.

proposals.title: Genus and Singularity Analysis of Curves from Permutation and Complete Mapping Polynomials over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields is central to cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. Recent work, including the determination of specific complete mapping forms, has been largely combinatorial and computational. A systematic geometric analysis of the algebraic curves defined by these functions is lacking, yet it can reveal deep structural properties governing their permutation behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the permutation property of a polynomial f over a finite field is intrinsically linked to the geometric properties—specifically the genus and singularity structure—of the associated curve y^q - y = f(x) or its related rational function curves.
proposals.method: We will construct plane algebraic curves C_f defined by equations of the form F(x, y) = 0, where F is derived from rational functions and permutation polynomials from recent papers (e.g., forms like aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+...). Using pre-2026 computational algebraic geometry tools (e.g., in SageMath or Magma), we will compute the genus via the Riemann-Hurwitz formula applied to relevant covers of the projective line, and classify singular points using local ring computations. The analysis will focus on how specific coefficient constraints that ensure permutation behavior simplify the singularity type and reduce the genus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new geometric framework for classifying permutation polynomials, moving beyond case-by-case algebraic manipulation. It will yield new necessary geometric conditions for permutation properties and potentially uncover infinite families of exceptional polynomials via low-genus or singular curve classifications.
proposals.novelty: While the arithmetic of zeta functions of curves over finite fields is classical, the direct application of genus and singularity theory to dissect the structure of modern permutation and complete mapping polynomials is a novel synthesis of algebraic geometry with finite field combinatorics.

proposals.title: Exceptional Multivariate Rational Functions and Permutation Polynomials over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: The theory of exceptional polynomials and rational functions over finite fields is central to constructing permutation polynomials, with deep connections to algebraic curves and the Carlitz-Wan conjecture. Recent work has focused intensely on univariate and low-degree multivariate forms, but a systematic study of multivariate rational functions, particularly their permutation and exceptional properties, remains underdeveloped. Scaling up these definitions is crucial for applications in cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs, where functions with predictable, structured mappings in higher dimensions are needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the definitions of exceptionality and permutation behavior to multivariate rational functions, we can identify new infinite families with desirable properties, and that these families can be characterized using connections to Galois theory and the geometry of algebraic surfaces over finite fields.
proposals.method: We will define multivariate rational functions as ratios of multivariate polynomials over 𝔽_q. The core method involves analyzing the associated function field extensions and their Galois groups to characterize exceptionality. For permutation properties, we will study the induced mappings on (𝔽_q)^n or (𝔽_{q^m})^n by examining the injectivity/surjectivity of the corresponding equations. Specific techniques will include Weil descent to relate multivariate functions to univariate ones over extension fields, and the construction of examples via symmetric polynomials, linearized polynomials, and monomials in several variables, followed by rigorous classification using algebraic criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for the study of multivariate exceptional and permutation rational functions. It will produce new explicit constructions of such functions, extending known univariate and binomial/trinomial results. The findings will contribute to the theoretical understanding of value sets and functional equations in several variables, with potential applications to the design of multi-dimensional cryptographic S-boxes and pseudorandom sequences.
proposals.novelty: While exceptional univariate polynomials are well-studied, the systematic extension to multivariate rational functions, combining the challenges of several variables and rational (non-polynomial) forms, is a novel and non-trivial direction. The approach synthesizes Ding's recent expertise in permutation polynomials, exceptional extensions, and complete mappings into a new, higher-dimensional setting.

proposals.title: Complete Classification of Exceptional Polynomials and Permutation Polynomials over GF(3) and GF(4)
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation polynomials (PPs) and exceptional polynomials over finite fields is central to cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. While asymptotic results and classifications over large fields exist, a systematic, exhaustive analysis over the smallest non-prime fields GF(3) and GF(4) is lacking. These minimal fields serve as critical base cases; their complete polynomial function maps can reveal fundamental structural constraints and counterexamples that inform broader conjectures, such as the Carlitz-Wan conjecture on exceptionality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a complete enumeration over GF(3) and GF(4) will yield a finite, classifiable set of permutation and exceptional polynomials, allowing for the discovery of new sporadic examples and the verification of known necessary conditions in their most stringent form.
proposals.method: We will perform an exhaustive computational search using SageMath (pre-2026 versions) to evaluate all polynomials of relevant degrees and forms over GF(3) and GF(4). For permutation polynomials, we will test bijectivity directly. For exceptional polynomials, we will implement the factorization-based definition, checking indecomposability over the algebraic closure. The search will be structured by degree bounds and will focus on the classes studied in Ding's recent work—specifically, sparse polynomials (e.g., pentanomials, trinomials of specific shapes like aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+...) and their extensions. Theoretical filtering using known criteria (e.g., Hermite's criterion, algebraic properties of function field extensions) will prune the search space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive catalog of permutation and exceptional polynomials over the smallest non-prime fields, serving as an indispensable reference for future theoretical work. It will test the sharpness of known degree bounds and may uncover minimal-degree counterexamples or new families that persist in larger fields, directly contributing to the understanding of the Carlitz-Wan conjecture and the construction of cryptographic functions.
proposals.novelty: While large-field asymptotics are well-studied, a complete, exhaustive classification for GF(3) and GF(4) has not been systematically documented, making this a foundational study. The novelty lies in using minimal fields as a rigorous proving ground to dissect the precise mechanisms of permutation and exceptional behavior in polynomial maps.

proposals.title: Characterization of Rational Functions Conjugate to Rédei Functions over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: Rédei functions, rational functions of the form (X^q + aX)/(X + a), are fundamental in finite field arithmetic for their exceptional properties, including being permutation polynomials and having simple algebraic structure. Classifying all rational functions conjugate to them—those of the form L ∘ R ∘ L⁻¹ for linear fractional L—would unify and extend known families of exceptional functions, which are crucial for constructing cryptographic mappings, complete mappings, and objects in coding theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the set of rational functions conjugate to classical Rédei functions can be completely characterized by a finite set of algebraic conditions on their coefficients, and that this family includes, but is not limited to, several known classes of permutation trinomials and pentanomials.
proposals.method: We will systematically analyze the conjugation action of the group PGL(2, F_q) on the set of rational functions of a specific degree form derived from Rédei functions. Using symbolic computation (e.g., in SageMath or Magma) to handle polynomial manipulations over finite fields, we will derive necessary and sufficient conditions on the coefficients of a rational function for it to be conjugate to a Rédei function. This involves solving systems of equations obtained by equating coefficients in the functional equation L(R(X)) = F(L(X)). The approach will leverage recent work on determining complete mappings of specific trinomial forms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a complete classification theorem for this important class of functions, generating new explicit families of permutation rational functions. It will deepen the understanding of exceptional covers of the projective line and may resolve special cases related to the Carlitz-Wan conjecture on exceptional polynomials by examining their rational function analogs.
proposals.novelty: While Rédei functions are well-studied, a full characterization of their conjugacy class under PGL(2) action is an open problem. The novelty lies in framing the search for exceptional functions as a conjugacy problem, linking classical objects to modern investigations on permutation polynomials and complete mappings via an explicit, algorithmic approach.

proposals.title: Spectral Characterization of Permutation Polynomials via Associated Linearized Operators
proposals.motivation: Permutation polynomials over finite fields are fundamental in cryptography and coding theory. While many specific classes have been constructed, a unified framework for analyzing their permutation behavior via spectral properties of associated linear operators is lacking. Such a framework could provide deeper structural insights and new criteria for permutation properties, moving beyond case-by-case algebraic manipulations.
proposals.hypothesis: The permutation property of a polynomial f(X) ∈ 𝔽_q[X] can be characterized by the spectral properties (eigenvalues, minimal polynomial, nilpotency) of the linear transformation induced by its associated linearized polynomial or a related matrix representation over 𝔽_q.
proposals.method: For a given polynomial f(X), we will construct its associated linearized polynomial L_f(Y) via the Carlitz module action or by analyzing the difference map f(X+a)-f(X)-f(a). We will then study the 𝔽_q-linear operator defined by L_f on a suitable extension field 𝔽_{q^n}, representing it as a matrix over 𝔽_q. Using tools from spectral theory (characteristic polynomial, invariant subspaces, Jordan form over the algebraic closure), we will derive necessary and sufficient conditions for f to be a permutation polynomial based on the invertibility and cycle structure of this operator.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel spectral-theoretic criterion for permutation polynomials, potentially unifying several known ad-hoc constructions. It may lead to new infinite families of permutation polynomials and provide algorithmic tools for testing permutation properties via linear algebra computations.
proposals.novelty: Applying the spectral theory of linear operators systematically to the study of permutation polynomials is a novel perspective. It moves beyond direct algebraic solving or using known theorems like Hermite's criterion, offering a matrix-analytic framework to dissect permutation behavior.

proposals.title: Permutation Properties of Polynomials with Coefficients in Extension Fields: Relaxing the Constant Term
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation polynomials (PPs) over finite fields is central to cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. Recent work, including Ding's own, has focused on characterizing PPs of specific forms, such as trinomials and pentanomials, with coefficients typically constrained to the base field F_q. This restriction simplifies analysis but may exclude families of PPs with useful algebraic properties or cryptographic robustness. Extending the coefficient domain to a proper extension field F_{q^m} is a natural generalization, motivated by applications in constructing complete mappings, orthogonal systems, and potentially more complex S-boxes for symmetric cryptography.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that relaxing the constant term c of certain low-weight permutation polynomial families (e.g., trinomials or the pentanomials studied by Ding) to lie in F_{q^m} will yield new, infinite families of permutation polynomials over F_{q^n}, and that the permutation conditions can be characterized by trace-like equations or norm conditions linking the extension fields F_{q^m} and F_{q^n}.
proposals.method: We will adopt and extend the algebraic method of investigating permutation properties via the associated rational functions, as used in Ding's recent papers. For a candidate polynomial f(X) = X^h + a X^r + c, with c in F_{q^m}, we will analyze the functional equation f(z)/f(z)^q = 1 for z in F_{q^n}*, translating the permutation condition over F_{q^n} into a system of equations involving the trace map from F_{q^m} to relevant subfields. We will systematically apply this to classes like X^{q+2}+cX and the pentanomial from Ding's 2025 paper, using the theory of exceptional polynomials and connections to the Carlitz-Wan conjecture to guide the search for necessary and sufficient conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for constructing permutation polynomials with coefficients in extension fields, expanding the known catalog of PPs. It will contribute new theoretical results on the interplay between finite field extensions and permutation criteria, potentially leading to new proofs or counterexamples related to the classification of exceptional polynomials. The results will be directly applicable to the design of new complete mappings and cryptographic primitives.
proposals.novelty: While permutation polynomials with coefficients in extension fields have been studied sporadically, a focused, systematic investigation of relaxing *only* the constant term in recently characterized low-weight PP families is novel. This approach leverages known base-field results as a springboard to discover new constructions, a non-obvious strategy given the typical focus on keeping all coefficients in the base field for simplicity.

proposals.title: Characterizing Complete Permutation Polynomials with Roots in the Base Field
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation polynomials (PPs) and complete mappings over finite fields is central to cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. A polynomial's root structure, particularly whether all its roots lie in the base field F_q, is a fundamental algebraic property that directly impacts its permutation behavior and its suitability for constructing other cryptographic primitives. Understanding this condition for known classes of PPs, such as those of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3, remains an open and practically significant problem.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given class of permutation polynomials over F_{q^2}, such as the complete mapping trinomials and pentanomials studied by the researcher, the condition that all roots lie in the base field F_q is equivalent to a specific, testable set of constraints on the polynomial's coefficients and the field characteristic.
proposals.method: We will employ algebraic and number-theoretic techniques grounded in the theory of finite fields. The core approach involves analyzing the polynomial f(X) over the extension field F_{q^2} by considering its factorization and using the property that α in F_{q^2} is in F_q if and only if α^q = α. This will translate the root condition into a system of equations in the coefficients. For specific forms (e.g., the complete mapping trinomial from recent work), we will use resultant computations and analyze the associated rational functions to derive explicit, coefficient-based criteria. The method will be extended to the permutation pentanomial classes the researcher has previously classified.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a complete theoretical characterization of when permutation polynomials from important families have all roots in the base field, a property not previously fully understood. It will yield concrete, checkable criteria that can be used to generate polynomials with desired root structures for applications. The results will also deepen the understanding of the interplay between a polynomial's permutation behavior and its algebraic structure.
proposals.novelty: While the permutation property of these polynomials has been studied, a systematic investigation of their root confinement to the base field is novel. The approach synthesizes the researcher's expertise in exceptional polynomials and complete mappings with a focused analysis on root localization, a distinct and underexplored angle.

proposals.title: Explicit Constructions of Exceptional Polynomials and Complete Mappings over Finite Field Extensions
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials over finite fields are central to finite geometry, coding theory, and cryptography due to their permutation properties. Recent work, including the resolution of the Carlitz-Wan conjecture, provides powerful theoretical criteria for exceptionality over global fields, but explicit, computable characterizations over specific finite extensions remain scarce. Specializing these high-level results to finite field extensions will yield concrete, applicable formulas for engineers and applied mathematicians.
proposals.hypothesis: The theoretical conditions for a polynomial to be exceptional or a complete mapping, when specialized to finite extensions F_{q^n}/F_q, will yield explicit, parameterized families of polynomials defined by solvable constraints on their coefficients over the base field.
proposals.method: We will systematically specialize the Galois-theoretic and valuation-theoretic criteria from 'Exceptional extensions of local fields...' to the case of extensions of finite fields. For permutation polynomials, we will apply these specialized criteria to the forms studied in recent papers (e.g., pentanomials and trinomials of specific shapes) to derive explicit inequalities and equations that their coefficients in F_q must satisfy. This will involve constructing algorithms in SageMath or Magma to test the resulting conditions over various (q, n) and classify all solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a catalog of explicit exceptional polynomials and complete mappings over finite field extensions, providing directly usable objects for applications. It will also demonstrate the computational power of the recently settled Carlitz-Wan theory by deriving its first concrete consequences for finite field polynomial constructions.
proposals.novelty: While the abstract theory of exceptional polynomials is mature, the systematic translation of the powerful Carlitz-Wan machinery into explicit, parameterized formulas for finite extensions is novel. Furthermore, applying this to complete mappings of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+... will solve a more constrained, applied problem not addressed in the original theoretical work.

proposals.title: Permutation Properties of Compositions of Rational Functions over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation rational functions is central to finite field theory, with applications in cryptography and coding. Research has focused on indecomposable functions, explicitly excluding those that are compositions. However, the permutation behavior of composed rational functions is poorly understood, creating a significant gap in the classification of all permutation rational functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic, non-trivial families of permutation rational functions exist that are compositions of simpler rational functions, and that their permutation properties can be characterized by the arithmetic of their component functions and the structure of the underlying field.
proposals.method: We will construct candidate functions as compositions F(X) = f(g(X)), where f and g are rational functions from Ding's recent work, such as low-degree polynomials and complete mappings. Using group-theoretic criteria (e.g., connections to exceptionality) and computational algebra in SageMath, we will test permutation properties over 𝔽_{q^n} for various n. The analysis will focus on how the cycle structure of the associated Galois groups for the function field extensions of f and g interacts under composition.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic classification of permutation rational functions that are non-trivial compositions, expanding the known families. It will provide new theoretical tools for analyzing composed maps and potentially yield new cryptographic primitives based on functional decomposition.
proposals.novelty: This directly relaxes the standard 'not a composition' indecomposability condition prevalent in the literature, turning a standard exclusion into a systematic object of study. The approach links the permutation property of a composition to the arithmetic of its components, a direction largely unexplored pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Complete Classification of Exceptional Polynomials of Degree n < 4096 over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials over finite fields are fundamental objects in number theory and algebraic geometry, with deep connections to permutation polynomials, finite geometry, and the inverse Galois problem. The classification of exceptional polynomials, particularly by degree, is a long-standing open problem. By 2025, significant progress has been made for specific low-degree families and forms, but a systematic classification for all degrees below a substantial bound like 4096 remains incomplete, leaving a gap in our understanding of these exceptional functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for each finite field 𝔽_q and each degree n with 1 < n < 4096, the set of exceptional polynomials over 𝔽_q of degree n can be completely determined and will consist of known constructions (like linearized, monomial, and Dickson-type polynomials) and a finite list of sporadic families derived from functional composition and rational transformations.
proposals.method: The approach will combine theoretical sieving with computational verification. First, we will leverage the known characterization linking exceptional polynomials to curves with infinitely many 𝔽_{q^i}-rational points, applying the Weil bound and results on Galois groups of polynomial decompositions to rule out vast classes of degrees. For the remaining candidate degrees and forms (particularly sparse polynomials like pentanomials and forms from recent papers), we will implement a targeted search algorithm in SageMath/PARI. This algorithm will test the exceptional property by checking the gcd condition gcd(P(x) - P(y), x - y) = 1 over 𝔽_q[t] for a sufficient set of prime powers q, using efficient polynomial gcd computations over function fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first exhaustive catalog of exceptional polynomials for degrees n < 4096, resolving the status for many open degrees. It will solidify the theoretical landscape and serve as a critical reference for applications in cryptography, coding theory, and the construction of combinatorial designs. The computational data may reveal new patterns or necessary conditions for exceptionality.
proposals.novelty: While previous works classify specific forms (e.g., permutation pentanomials) or low-degree cases, the systematic, degree-bounded classification up to 4096 for all finite fields and all polynomial forms is novel. The integration of theoretical constraints from arithmetic geometry with large-scale, form-specific computational searches tailored to recent results on sparse representations is a distinct methodological advance.

proposals.title: Complete Classification of Exceptional Polynomials of Degrees 8 and 32 over Finite Fields for All Prime Powers q
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials over finite fields are central to constructing permutation polynomials, with applications in cryptography and coding theory. Prior classifications for degrees 8 and 32, often relying on the classification of finite simple groups (CFSG), typically include a 'sufficiently large q' condition to avoid intricate case analysis for small fields. This gap leaves the complete theoretical picture and practical utility for all finite fields incomplete.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining refined Galois-theoretic arguments, explicit computation of factorization patterns over small fields, and a systematic analysis of the remaining small-q cases, the 'sufficiently large q' condition can be eliminated, yielding a definitive list of exceptional polynomials of these degrees for all prime powers q.
proposals.method: The approach will first leverage the established connection between exceptionality and Galois groups of the polynomial over global function fields. We will use the document of known exceptional polynomials and the theory of arithmetic monodromy groups. For the finite set of small q where the general CFSG-based argument fails, we will implement a computational algorithm in SageMath or Magma. This algorithm will test the exceptionality condition—that the polynomial is a permutation polynomial over F_q^n for infinitely many n—by directly checking its factorization and gcd conditions over the rings of rational functions F_q[t] for a targeted set of small parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete, unconditional classification of exceptional polynomials of degrees 8 and 32, closing a known gap in the literature. It will solidify the theoretical foundation for constructing permutation polynomials and may reveal new sporadic examples over very small fields, with potential implications for the design of cryptographic functions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of theoretical Galois-theoretic constraints with a targeted, exhaustive computational verification for the problematic small-q cases, a non-trivial step avoided in prior asymptotic analyses. This moves the result from asymptotic to definitive.

proposals.title: Counting Permutation Rational Functions via Symbolic Summation and Exceptionality Criteria
proposals.motivation: Counting permutation polynomials and rational functions over finite fields is a fundamental problem with applications in cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. Current methods often rely on ad-hoc constructions or exhaustive searches for specific low-degree forms, lacking a systematic, algorithmic framework for broader classes. Symbolic summation, a powerful tool in computer algebra for handling combinatorial identities, offers a promising but largely unexplored approach to this enumeration problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the property of a rational function being a permutation can be encoded as a logical condition on its coefficients, and that the subsequent counting problem over finite fields can be formulated and solved algorithmically using symbolic summation techniques over polynomial rings.
proposals.method: We will formalize the permutation condition for rational functions of the form f(X)/g(X) in F_q(X) using the characterization via exceptionality and the associated curve (f(X)-f(Y))/(X-Y). This condition translates into a system of polynomial equations in the coefficients. Using the holonomic systems approach (e.g., as implemented in the Sigma package), we will treat the counting function N(q, d) for a fixed degree pattern as a sum over the solution set of this system. The core technical step is to derive and solve recurrence relations or closed-form expressions for N(q, d) by applying creative telescoping to the symbolic sums representing the count.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general algorithmic framework for counting permutation rational functions of prescribed forms, moving beyond case-by-case analysis. It will yield new explicit counting formulas for families of permutation rational functions and potentially uncover new infinite families through the analytic structure of the derived recurrences.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of the theory of exceptional polynomials/rational functions with the algorithmic machinery of symbolic summation represents a significant methodological shift. While both areas are mature pre-2026, their combined application to enumeration problems in finite fields is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Group-Theoretic Analysis of Permutation Polynomials: Compositional Structure and Exceptionality
proposals.motivation: The classification of permutation polynomials (PPs) over finite fields is a central problem in finite fields and their applications in cryptography and coding theory. Recent work, including the determination of specific complete mappings and permutation pentanomials, has produced rich families of PPs, but their deeper algebraic structure remains unexplored. A systematic group-theoretic study of the permutation groups generated by these polynomials can reveal hidden symmetries and compositional properties, linking concrete constructions to abstract group theory.
proposals.hypothesis: The permutation groups generated by specific, well-studied classes of permutation polynomials (such as those of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3 and certain pentanomials) possess constrained compositional structures—such as being subgroups of specific classical groups or having particular cycle type distributions—that explain and predict their exceptional permutation behavior.
proposals.method: We will first compute the permutation groups G_f = ⟨f, affine permutations⟩ generated by iterated composition of a target polynomial f (from the cited classes) with all affine permutations over 𝔽_{q^n}. Using computational algebra systems (e.g., GAP, Magma) for small to medium fields, we will classify these groups by order, primitivity, and isomorphism type. This data will guide a theoretical analysis, employing tools from the classification of finite linear groups and character sums, to prove general constraints on the group structure for infinite families of parameters, linking properties like exceptionality of the polynomial to the group being 'large' (e.g., containing PSL(2,q)) or 'small' (solvable).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel group-theoretic framework for analyzing permutation polynomials, moving beyond ad-hoc constructions. It will provide new invariants (group isomorphism type) for PPs and potentially yield new proofs or generalizations of results like the Carlitz-Wan conjecture for exceptional polynomials by interpreting them via group actions. The classification tables generated will be a valuable resource for constructing polynomials with desired symmetric properties.
proposals.novelty: While the group generated by a single permutation polynomial is a known object, its systematic computation and classification for recently discovered parametric families is entirely novel. The explicit connection between the algebraic form of a polynomial (e.g., a sparse multivariate Laurent polynomial type) and the structure of its generated permutation group has not been investigated in the literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Enumerating Permutation Polynomials from a Complete Mapping Construction over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: The systematic construction and enumeration of permutation polynomials (PPs) over finite fields is a core problem in finite fields and their applications in cryptography and coding theory. While specific classes, like the complete mappings of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3 over F_{q^2}, have been characterized, the exact count of distinct PPs generated by such parametric families remains largely unexplored. Determining this count is crucial for assessing the richness and utility of these constructions for generating pseudorandom sequences or S-boxes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the number of distinct permutation polynomials produced by the parametric construction aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3 over F_{q^2} can be expressed as a closed-form function of the field size q and the constraints on the parameters (a,b,c,d) derived from the known characterization theorems.
proposals.method: We will build directly on the characterization theorems from Ding's recent paper, which provide precise conditions on (a,b,c,d) in F_{q^2} for the polynomial to be a complete mapping (and thus a PP). The core method is combinatorial enumeration: for a fixed q, we will analyze the solution sets of the defining algebraic constraints over the finite field. This involves counting distinct quadruples (a,b,c,d) up to equivalence (e.g., scaling) that satisfy the conditions, using tools from the theory of equations over finite fields, orbit-stabilizer theorems for group actions (like multiplication by non-zero scalars), and computational verification for small primes using software like SageMath.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit formulas or tight asymptotic bounds for the number of PPs from this important class, quantifying the generative power of the construction. It will bridge the gap between existential characterization and quantitative enumeration, offering a concrete resource for applications requiring large sets of PPs.
proposals.novelty: While the polynomials themselves have been characterized, the explicit enumeration of all distinct instances is a novel combinatorial problem derived from this characterization. The approach moves beyond proving existence to providing a precise census, a step not taken in the prior analytical work.

proposals.title: Characterizing Exceptional Polynomials via Generalized Rédei Function Constructions
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials over finite fields are central to finite geometry, coding theory, and cryptography due to their permutation properties over infinitely many extensions. The classification of such polynomials remains incomplete. Recent work on permutation polynomials, particularly those of specific forms like $aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3$, suggests a deep connection with rational function constructions akin to Rédei functions, which are known to generate permutation polynomials. A systematic study of which exceptional polynomials arise from such a generalized construction is a natural and important next step.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant class of exceptional polynomials, particularly those of the form $X^r h(X^{(q-1)/d})$ and related multivariate factorizations, can be characterized as specializations or functional compositions derived from a generalized Rédei-type rational function construction over $\mathbb{F}_q(t)$.
proposals.method: We will define a generalized Rédei construction starting from a bivariate polynomial $G(X,t)$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$, whose iterates yield rational functions. By studying the factorization of $G(X,t)-G(Y,t)$ over the algebraic closure, we will derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the associated polynomials to be exceptional. This involves analyzing the Galois groups of the equations $G(X,t)=a$ and applying the classification of exceptional covers via the monodromy groups and the Carlitz-Wan theory. The specific forms from recent papers will serve as test cases and guides for parameterizing $G(X,t)$.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unifying framework for constructing and classifying exceptional polynomials, linking geometric methods (Galois theory of covers) with explicit polynomial identities. It will yield new infinite families of exceptional polynomials and potentially complete the classification for several low-degree or low-parameter families, advancing the understanding of permutation rational functions.
proposals.novelty: While Rédei functions are classical, their systematic exploitation for characterizing *exceptional* polynomials, rather than just constructing individual permutation examples, is novel. The approach synthesizes Ding's recent work on specific permutation forms with the deep, pre-2026 theory of exceptional covers and the Carlitz-Wan conjecture.

proposals.title: Character Sums and Group Actions: A Unified Framework for Proving Permutation Properties of Multinomials over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: The construction and verification of permutation polynomials (PPs) over finite fields is a central problem in finite field theory with applications in cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. While many specific families, like those in Ding's recent work, have been studied via ad-hoc algebraic manipulations, a more systematic, group-theoretic framework for proving permutation properties is lacking. Such a framework could unify disparate results and streamline the discovery of new classes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the permutation property of multinomials of the form $\sum_i a_i X^{e_i}$ over $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ can be characterized and proven by analyzing the structure of associated character sums, particularly by leveraging the action of the Galois group $\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q)$ and the orthogonality relations of multiplicative and additive characters.
proposals.method: The core technical approach is to associate to a candidate polynomial $F(X)$ a family of exponential sums, such as $\sum_{x \in \mathbb{F}_{q^n}^*} \chi(F(x)/x)$, where $\chi$ is a multiplicative character. We will employ the group action of the Galois group to partition the field elements into orbits, simplifying the sum evaluation. For specific forms (e.g., $aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3$), we will derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the vanishing of these sums for all non-trivial characters, which is equivalent to the permutation property. This involves manipulating rational functions in $X$ and $X^{q}$ and applying theorems like the Hasse-Davenport lifting formula.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, group-theoretic toolkit for proving permutation properties, moving beyond case-by-case factorization. It will yield new, verifiable criteria for several multinomial families, potentially leading to the discovery of new permutation polynomials. The framework will also deepen the theoretical connection between permutation polynomials, character sums, and Galois theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically applying Galois group actions and character sum orthogonality as a primary proof mechanism for permutation polynomials, rather than as an auxiliary tool, creating a unified method applicable to the complex multinomial structures central to Ding's recent research.

proposals.title: Systematic Derivation of Permutation Trinomials from Known Complete Mapping Constructions over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: Permutation polynomials, especially those with few terms, are fundamental in cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. Trinomials are of particular interest due to their simple form and efficient evaluation, but constructing and proving new infinite families remains a significant challenge. Recent work by Ding and others on complete mappings and permutation pentanomials provides a rich source of complex polynomials whose structural constraints may yield simpler permutation subcases.
proposals.hypothesis: The complete mapping construction of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3 over F_{q^2} contains, as a special subfamily, an infinite class of permutation trinomials over F_{q^2} when specific algebraic relations are imposed on the coefficients a, b, c, d.
proposals.method: We will analyze the known condition for the complete mapping property, derived via the multivariate method and Hermite's criterion. By imposing constraints that force two of the four coefficients to vanish or become functionally dependent, we will reduce the polynomial to a trinomial. The core technical step is to solve the resulting system of equations over F_q, ensuring the permutation condition is preserved. This involves symbolic computation (using packages like SageMath) to handle parameterized equations and Gröbner basis techniques to classify all viable coefficient sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce new explicit infinite families of permutation trinomials over finite fields of even and odd characteristic, expanding the known catalog. It will also demonstrate a novel method for deriving sparse permutation polynomials from more complex, parameterized constructions, providing a template for future discoveries.
proposals.novelty: While the parent complete mapping is known, the systematic extraction and classification of its permutation trinomial subfamilies has not been undertaken. The novelty lies in the targeted parameter reduction within a proven framework to generate new, verifiably simple permutation polynomials.

proposals.title: Galois-Equivariant Descent for Permutation Polynomials and Complete Mappings over All Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: Many explicit constructions in finite fields, such as permutation polynomials and complete mappings, are first established over prime fields F_p. Generalizing these constructions to all finite fields F_{q^n} (q = p^m) is a fundamental and non-trivial problem with applications in cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. Existing methods often rely on ad-hoc searches or specific forms, lacking a unifying principle for systematic extension.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a construction over a prime field F_p to be explicitly equivariant under the action of the Frobenius automorphism, one can apply the machinery of Galois descent to naturally and rigorously extend its validity to all finite extensions F_{q^n}, thereby proving its properties universally.
proposals.method: We will take specific classes of functions over F_p (e.g., permutation pentanomials or complete mappings of the form aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3) from recent work. The core technical step is to reinterpret these functions as being defined on the F_p-vector space F_{p^n} and then impose Galois-equivariance conditions relative to the cyclic group generated by the Frobenius map σ(x)=x^p. This involves solving for parameter constraints that ensure the function commutes with σ, or 'twisting' the base construction by σ-actions. The proof will leverage the fact that a polynomial defined over F_p that permutes F_{p^n} must be a permutation polynomial over all intermediate fields if it is compatible with the Galois action.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a powerful and general algebraic framework for lifting constructions from prime fields to all finite fields, moving beyond case-by-case analysis. It will yield new infinite families of permutation polynomials and complete mappings with proven properties, and potentially resolve the status of conjectured exceptional polynomials in broader settings.
proposals.novelty: While Galois descent is a known technique in algebra, its systematic application to the concrete, explicit construction of combinatorial objects over finite fields—translating a crystallography-inspired analogy into a rigorous mathematical procedure—is novel. This approach unifies and explains previously disparate generalizations.

proposals.title: Exceptional Polynomials and Complete Mappings over Arbitrary Finite Fields: A Unified Construction
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Ding and others has produced significant results on permutation polynomials and complete mappings over prime fields and fields of the form $\mathbb{F}_{q^2}$. However, many constructions and classifications are restricted to specific field characteristics or extensions, limiting their applicability in cryptography and combinatorial design. A unified theory for arbitrary finite fields $\mathbb{F}_{q}$, where $q$ is any prime power, is a natural and important next step.
proposals.hypothesis: The structural constraints and functional equations that characterize exceptional polynomials and complete mappings over prime fields and quadratic extensions can be generalized, leading to explicit, parameterized families valid over all finite fields.
proposals.method: We will systematically extend the analysis of forms like $aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+cX^{q+2}+dX^3$ from $\mathbb{F}_{q^2}$ to arbitrary extensions $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ using the theory of linearized polynomials and their associates. The core technique involves studying the permutation and value set properties via Weil bounds and character sums, as in the proof of the Carlitz-Wan conjecture for exceptional polynomials. We will develop algorithms to test and classify candidate polynomials by solving systems of equations over the base field derived from the condition that the associated rational function induces a permutation on all subfields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive families of exceptional permutation polynomials and complete mappings explicitly defined for all finite fields, removing the characteristic or extension-degree restrictions present in prior literature. It will offer new tools for constructing cryptographic functions and orthogonal Latin squares over a wider range of parameters.
proposals.novelty: While previous results are piecemeal for specific $q$ or $n$, this project aims for a complete, parameterized characterization. The key novelty lies in adapting the 'exceptional' methodology, previously linked to the Carlitz-Wan conjecture, to the problem of constructing complete mappings over non-prime, non-quadratic extensions in a systematic way.

proposals.title: A Lefschetz-Type Criterion for Permutation Polynomials on Projective Spaces via Lattice Automorphisms
proposals.motivation: Characterizing permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields is a central problem with applications in cryptography and coding theory. Recent work by Ding and others has focused on explicit constructions, particularly for specific forms over extension fields. A unifying theoretical framework to classify such functions, especially on projective spaces, remains elusive. This project addresses this gap by importing conceptual tools from algebraic topology to finite geometry.
proposals.hypothesis: A function f on the projective space P(F_q^n) is a bijection if and only if its induced map on the lattice of linear subspaces is an automorphism and the sequence of its fixed-point counts on iterates satisfies a combinatorial congruence derived from the zeta function of the space.
proposals.method: We will define discrete invariants by studying the action of a rational function f on the lattice of F_q-linear subspaces of F_q^n and its associated étale cohomology (l-adic). Using Weil cohomology theory, we will formulate a finite-field Lefschetz fixed-point formula for iterates of f. The core algorithm will involve computing the characteristic polynomial of the induced map on a suitable cohomology module and verifying congruence conditions between the trace of its powers and the point counts of f^k on P(F_q^n). This will be tested on known classes of permutation polynomials and exceptional polynomials.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new cohomological criterion for permutation properties on projective spaces, potentially unifying several known results. It will also introduce novel methods from arithmetic geometry into the study of finite field functions, opening avenues for classifying more complex mappings.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in translating the Lefschetz fixed-point theorem, a cornerstone of algebraic topology, into an effective algebraic-combinatorial test for bijections in finite geometry. It moves beyond ad-hoc construction to a structural, invariant-based classification.

proposals.title: Analytic and Algebraic Characterization of Bijective Dimensions for Polynomial Maps on Projective Spaces over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: The study of permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields is central to cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorics. Recent work, including the determination of specific complete mappings, has focused on establishing exact algebraic conditions for bijectivity on affine or projective spaces for fixed extension degrees. However, a global understanding of how bijectivity behaves as the dimension n varies is lacking. A statistical theory describing the set of dimensions for which a map permutes P(F_q^n) would reveal deeper structural properties of these algebraic dynamical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given family of rational maps or homogeneous polynomial maps on projective spaces, the set of dimensions n for which the map induces a bijection is determined by an arithmetic condition, and this set possesses a well-defined analytic density, often zero, describable via Dirichlet series or sieve methods.
proposals.method: First, for a target family (e.g., forms like aX^{3q}+bX^{2q+1}+... lifted to projective lines or higher-dimensional spaces), we will derive exact algebraic criteria for bijectivity on P(F_{q^n}) using tools from algebraic geometry over finite fields and the study of exceptional polynomials. This yields an arithmetic function a(n), where a(n)=1 if the map is bijective. Second, we will analyze the summatory function of a(n) using analytic number theory.
