We will construct associated Dirichlet series and apply Tauberian theorems, or use sieve techniques if the condition is of the form 'n not in a union of arithmetic progressions,' to determine the asymptotic density and distribution of bijective dimensions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first analytic framework for counting bijective dimensions, moving beyond case-by-case algebraic verification. It will establish theorems on the sparsity or prevalence of such dimensions, connecting algebraic dynamics to analytic number theory. The results will offer a new classification principle for polynomial maps based on their 'bijection spectrum.'
proposals.novelty: The fusion of exact algebraic bijection criteria with analytic density calculations for varying extension degree n is novel. While the algebraic theory of exceptional polynomials is established, its systematic translation into an analytic arithmetic function for projective maps has not been undertaken.

proposals.title: Dynamical Heights and Galois Bounds for Exceptional Loci of Rational Self-Maps
proposals.motivation: In arithmetic dynamics, the study of preperiodic and exceptional subvarieties is central to understanding the global structure of dynamical systems on algebraic varieties. Pre-2026, the classification of such loci for specific maps (e.g., permutation polynomials) is well-developed, but a quantitative, Galois-theoretic analysis of their arithmetic complexity is largely unexplored. This project bridges this gap by applying the machinery of canonical heights and Galois theory to exceptional loci, aiming to stratify varieties based on the iterative arithmetic behavior of their special subvarieties.
proposals.hypothesis: The exceptional loci of a rational self-map on a variety exhibit constrained arithmetic dynamics, characterized by bounded growth of canonical heights and Galois groups of iterated preimage fields, which can be used to define a new dynamical stratification of the variety.
proposals.method: We will construct explicit families of rational self-maps on projective varieties, building on the researcher's expertise with polynomials over finite fields and exceptional extensions. For each map, we will compute canonical heights on its exceptional subvarieties using local height algorithms. Simultaneously, we will analyze the Galois groups of field extensions generated by iterated preimages of points on these loci, leveraging techniques from the Carlitz-Wan conjecture work. This dual quantitative-Galois analysis will be synthesized to define invariants measuring arithmetic dynamical complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new framework for quantifying the arithmetic complexity of exceptional loci, leading to conjectures linking dynamical Mordell-Lang problems with equidistribution. It will yield concrete bounds on height growth and Galois uniformity for families of maps, extending the theory of unlikely intersections into a dynamical context.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of quantitative arithmetic dynamics (heights, orbit uniformity) with the structural study of exceptional loci provides a novel, non-obvious perspective. Moving from classification to measuring iterative arithmetic complexity on these special subvarieties represents a significant shift from existing pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Definability and Stability of Exceptional Covers in Pseudo-Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: Exceptional covers of the projective line, which are Galois covers remaining irreducible over infinitely many finite fields, are central objects in arithmetic geometry and the inverse Galois problem. Their classification, often approached via permutation polynomials and rational functions, remains incomplete. The model theory of pseudo-finite fields, a stable theory with a well-understood definable set structure, provides a powerful but underexplored framework for analyzing such geometric families defined over finite fields.
proposals.hypothesis: Families of exceptional covers, when parametrized over finite fields, form definable sets in the theory of pseudo-finite fields, and their geometric exceptionality is encoded in model-theoretic properties such as Morley rank and orthogonality to the fixed field of the Frobenius automorphism.
proposals.method: We will formalize families of covers, given by equations of the form f(X,Y)=0, as definable sets in the language of rings. Using the quantifier elimination and stability properties of pseudo-finite fields, we will analyze the definability of the set of parameters for which the cover is exceptional. Specific techniques include computing the dimension and rank of these definable sets, and studying their interaction with the canonical automorphism (Frobenius) in enriched structures. This will involve translating combinatorial criteria for exceptionality (involving permutation rational functions) into first-order formulas.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new model-theoretic classification of exceptional covers, revealing constraints on their defining equations via definability and rank. It will establish a bridge between arithmetic geometry and model theory, offering new invariants for the Grothendieck-Teichmüller program's study of fundamental groups and covers over finite fields.
proposals.novelty: While model theory has been applied to finite and pseudo-finite fields, its systematic use to classify exceptional covers—a core topic in Ding's recent work on permutation polynomials and complete mappings—is novel. The fusion of definability analysis with the arithmetic of exceptionality provides a fundamentally new lens.

proposals.title: Quantifying Rarity: Analytic Densities of Exceptional Galois Covers and Their Monodromy Groups
proposals.motivation: The classification of exceptional covers of the projective line, central to the Guralnick-Thompson conjecture and its refinements, is a deep structural result in inverse Galois theory. However, it remains a qualitative statement. A natural quantitative question, largely unexplored before 2026, is: how rare are these exceptional covers among all Galois covers when ordered by a natural arithmetic parameter (e.g., the discriminant of the associated field extension)? Answering this provides a statistical lens on the landscape of Galois groups in geometric families.
proposals.hypothesis: The natural density of parameter points yielding exceptional Galois covers is zero for monodromy groups belonging to most families of finite simple groups, but may be positive for a very restricted subset of groups related to low-genus and low-multiplicity ramification types, with the density function revealing constraints on the associated arithmetic monodromy groups.
proposals.method: We will define parameter spaces for covers (e.g., via coefficients of defining polynomials over number fields of bounded discriminant) and employ analytic number theory techniques, specifically sieve methods and generating series, to count covers up to a discriminant bound. The core technical step is to translate the geometric condition of being 'exceptional'—via the rigidity criterion and the classification of finite simple groups—into an arithmetic condition on the parameters. This will allow us to bound the number of exceptional covers using character sum estimates over function fields and results on the factorization of polynomials. The analysis will be stratified by the associated monodromy group type.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative density estimates for exceptional covers, moving beyond pure classification. It will establish novel theorems linking the analytic properties of density functions (e.g., convergence rates) to the group-theoretic structure of monodromy, offering a new statistical invariant for Galois-theoretic families. The results will also provide concrete asymptotic bounds relevant to the enumeration of special covers in computational Galois theory.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of quantitative, analytic-number-theoretic density estimation with the qualitative, group-theoretic classification of exceptional covers is a novel synthesis. Pre-2026 work on exceptional covers focuses on existence and classification, not on their statistical prevalence within natural ensembles, making this a new direction in arithmetic statistics.

proposals.title: Definability and Enumeration: A Model-Theoretic Study of Exceptional Polynomials of Low Degree
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials, which are permutation polynomials over infinitely many finite extensions, are central to finite field theory and have applications in cryptography and coding theory. Their classification, even within low-degree families like permutation trinomials and pentanomials, remains incomplete and often relies on ad-hoc algebraic criteria. Model theory offers powerful tools like quantifier elimination and definability to understand the logical structure of mathematical properties, but these have rarely been applied to concrete, computationally generated data in finite field arithmetic.
proposals.hypothesis: Within specific, parametrized families of low-degree polynomials (e.g., trinomials, pentanomials), the subset of exceptional polynomials is definable in the language of rings, and its definitional complexity can be precisely characterized using the concrete enumeration data as a testbed.
proposals.method: We will first algorithmically enumerate all polynomials of a given low-degree form (e.g., X^n + aX^m + bX) over initial finite fields F_q, using permutation testing to identify exceptional candidates, building explicit databases. This finite data will be treated as a structure in the language of rings. We will then apply model-theoretic techniques, such as seeking quantifier-free or existential definitions for the 'exceptional' property within the family, using the database to refute candidate definitions. Tools from stability theory will be used to analyze the complexity of any successful definition.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce new, provably complete lists of exceptional polynomials in low-degree families and establish rigorous logical characterizations of their exceptionality. It will create a novel methodology bridging exhaustive computation and abstract model theory, potentially leading to new algorithms for certifying exceptional polynomials and a deeper understanding of their intrinsic algebraic complexity.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of exhaustive computational enumeration with model-theoretic definability analysis for exceptional polynomials is a novel interdisciplinary approach. It moves beyond pure algebraic classification to ask fundamental questions about the logical expressibility of a key arithmetic property.

proposals.title: A Gross-Zagier Formula for Non-isotrivial Rank 1 Drinfeld Modules: Heights, L-series Derivatives, and Rational Points
proposals.motivation: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer (BSD) conjecture for Drinfeld modules, especially in the non-isotrivial case, remains a major open problem in arithmetic geometry over function fields. While the Gross-Zagier formula for elliptic curves over number fields is a cornerstone for studying BSD, its direct analogue for Drinfeld modules is undeveloped, leaving a critical gap in the toolkit for constructing rational points and investigating analytic ranks in positive characteristic.
proposals.hypothesis: For a non-isotrivial rank 1 Drinfeld module over a global function field, the derivative of its L-function at a central critical point can be expressed as a product of a canonical height of a specially constructed Heegner-type point and an explicit non-zero local factor.
proposals.method: We will replace the classical modular curve with the Drinfeld modular curve for GL(2) over a function field, using its rigid-analytic uniformization. Heegner points will be constructed via complex multiplication by Hayes modules (the function field analogue of CM elliptic curves). The analytic kernel will be built using harmonic analysis on the Bruhat-Tits tree for PGL(2, F) (where F is a local field) and Rankin-Selberg integrals for automorphic forms on GL(2), adapting the pre-2026 work of Pál and others on automorphic forms over function fields. The height pairing will be computed using the intersection theory of Drinfeld modular curves as developed by Gekeler.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit Gross-Zagier formula in the non-isotrivial Drinfeld module setting, offering a concrete, computable method to generate rational points of infinite order. It will establish a vital new link between analytic and algebraic invariants, directly advancing the BSD conjecture for rank 1 Drinfeld modules.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic adversarial redesign of the entire Gross-Zagier architecture—replacing classical objects with their Drinfeld module and function field counterparts—specifically to attack the non-isotrivial BSD problem, a setting where prior generalizations fail or are vacuous.

proposals.title: Permutation Polynomials and Complete Mappings on Global Function Fields: Constructions and Exceptionality
proposals.motivation: Recent work on permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields, including Ding's own results on specific forms, has been crucial for applications in cryptography, coding theory, and combinatorial designs. However, these constructions are largely confined to the base field F_q or its finite extensions. Generalizing these problems to the setting of global function fields (algebraic function fields over F_q) offers a richer geometric and arithmetic structure, potentially yielding new infinite families of permutation objects and deepening the understanding of exceptionality in a broader context, analogous to the extension from local to global fields seen in the Carlitz-Wan conjecture.
proposals.hypothesis: That the techniques for constructing and classifying permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields can be systematically lifted to the rings of regular functions on affine curves (coordinate rings of global function fields), producing new, infinite families of permutation rational functions and yielding a geometric interpretation of exceptionality.
proposals.method: We will first define permutation properties for rational function fields and their finite extensions, focusing on the ring of functions regular outside a fixed place (a 'S-integer' ring). Using Ding's recent expertise with specific polynomial forms (e.g., trinomials, pentanomials), we will develop analogous constructions by treating the variable X as a generator of a separable extension of function fields. The core technical approach involves analyzing the associated maps on divisor classes and their reduction modulo various places, employing tools from the theory of Drinfeld modules and Carlitz extensions where applicable, to establish necessary and sufficient conditions for permutation behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new framework for studying permutation rational functions on algebraic curves, moving beyond the classical finite field setting. It will produce explicit, parameterized families of permutation constructions on global function fields and provide a function-field analogue to the theory of exceptional polynomials, potentially revealing new connections with the arithmetic of the Jacobian.
proposals.novelty: While permutation polynomials over finite fields are well-studied, a systematic investigation of their analogues on higher-dimensional function field extensions is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in scaling the combinatorial problem to a geometric arena, linking Ding's concrete polynomial classification work to the arithmetic geometry of curves.

proposals.title: Global Exceptional Covers: A Local-to-Global Classification via Ramification and Rationality
proposals.motivation: Exceptional extensions of local fields, characterized by having few distinct extensions for a given Galois group and inertia, are rare and deeply studied, with recent progress on the Carlitz-Wan conjecture. In global arithmetic geometry, constructing Galois covers of the projective line with prescribed local behavior is a central challenge, linking the inverse Galois problem to anabelian geometry. This project aims to bridge these areas by systematically studying global covers that are locally exceptional at a dense set of places, a condition expected to impose severe geometric constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: A connected, smooth projective curve over a global field K that provides a Galois cover of the projective line and yields an exceptional extension at a density-one set of primes of K must belong to a finite, explicitly classifiable list of geometric types, severely constraining its genus, Galois group, and ramification structure.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged approach combining arithmetic geometry and computational algebra. First, we will use the theory of fundamental groups and Hurwitz spaces to parametrize covers with a given monodromy group and ramification type over ℚ or 𝔽_q(t). Second, we will impose the local exceptionality condition by analyzing the reduction of these covers modulo primes, leveraging known classifications of local exceptional extensions (e.g., from work on the Carlitz-Wan conjecture) to derive Diophantine constraints on the moduli. This will involve studying the rationality of defining equations (polynomials, rational functions) over the global field and their behavior under specialization.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a classification theorem for globally defined covers with ubiquitous local exceptionality, yielding new examples or non-existence results. It will establish a novel local-to-global principle for Galois covers and provide new insights into the geometric realizability of certain Galois groups with constrained ramification, advancing the interplay between anabelian geometry and inverse Galois theory.
proposals.novelty: While local exceptionality and global covers are studied separately, the systematic fusion—demanding a cover be locally exceptional at almost all places—is a new and powerful global constraint. The method of applying the complete classification of local exceptional extensions to constrain global moduli spaces is an unexplored synthesis of arithmetic and geometric techniques.

proposals.title: Lifting Exceptionality: A p-adic Framework for Permutation Polynomials over Local Rings
proposals.motivation: The theory of exceptional polynomials, including permutation polynomials, is a cornerstone of finite field theory with applications in coding and cryptography. However, its scope is fundamentally restricted to fields. Generalizing this theory to rings, particularly local rings like the p-adic integers ℤ_p, would create a powerful bridge to number theory and arithmetic geometry, enabling the study of polynomial mappings in a richer topological-algebraic context where approximation and lifting techniques are natural.
proposals.hypothesis: That the exceptional (e.g., permutation) behavior of a polynomial over a finite field 𝔽_q can be systematically lifted to the ring of p-adic integers ℤ_p (or its finite extensions) under precise conditions governed by p-adic analysis and Hensel-type criteria, thereby defining a robust notion of exceptionality over local rings.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the base case: a polynomial f(X) ∈ ℤ_p[X] reduced modulo p must be an exceptional permutation polynomial over the residue field 𝔽_q. Using Hensel's Lemma, we will develop explicit p-adic criteria—involving the non-vanishing of derivatives modulo p and bounds on p-adic valuations—to lift injectivity/surjectivity from the residue field to the local ring. The core technical work involves analyzing the iterative p-adic Newton lifting process for the equation f(x)=f(y) to establish global injectivity, adapting methods from the study of power series and dynamical systems over p-adic fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first systematic framework for defining and studying exceptional polynomials over complete local rings. It will produce new, infinite families of permutation mappings over rings and provide a novel p-adic topological perspective on a classical algebraic combinatorics problem, potentially revealing connections to Galois theory and arithmetic dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While exceptional polynomials over finite fields are well-studied, and Hensel's Lemma is standard, their synthesis to define a lifted exceptionality over ℤ_p is unexplored. The novelty lies in formulating the precise lifting conditions and proving that the global permutation property can be ensured by a local (p-adic) criterion, an analogy to stability in topological dynamics.

proposals.title: Exceptional Polynomials over Local Rings and Dedekind Domains: A Local-Global Rigidity Conjecture
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials over finite fields, which induce permutation polynomials over infinitely many extensions, are central to finite field theory, cryptography, and coding theory. The Carlitz-Wan conjecture (proven by Fried, Guralnick, and Saxl) established that such polynomials over finite fields are rare and highly structured. Extending this study to polynomials over more general coefficient rings (e.g., rings of integers, local rings) connects arithmetic dynamics to inverse Galois theory and the deformation of Galois covers, a natural progression in post-Carlitz-Wan research.
proposals.hypothesis: A polynomial over a local ring or Dedekind domain that induces an exceptional extension of local fields for infinitely many residue characteristics must be a base change of a classical exceptional polynomial over a finite field. This rigidity fails for more general rings, and the precise ring-theoretic conditions for failure can be classified.
proposals.method: The approach combines arithmetic geometry and explicit computation. First, we will analyze the structure of permutation polynomials and complete mappings over rings like ℤ/p^kℤ and 𝔽_q[[t]], building on Ding's recent work on specific forms. Second, we will employ deformation theory for branched covers to study families of polynomials over these rings. Third, we will implement algorithms to test exceptionality by checking permutation behavior over finite quotients of the ring, using symbolic computation in SageMath or Magma to search for novel counterexamples over non-classical rings.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new local-global principle for exceptional polynomials, generalizing the Carlitz-Wan theory. It will produce the first systematic classification of coefficient rings where the classical finite-field models are forced, and construct explicit examples of 'exceptional' polynomials over rings that do not descend to finite fields, enriching arithmetic dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the classical direction: instead of starting from a finite field, we begin with a polynomial over a general ring and ask when its local behavior forces a finite-field origin. This fusion of deformation theory with the explicit study of permutation polynomials over rings is unexplored.

proposals.title: Exceptionality for Inseparable Polynomials: Constructions, Classifications, and Applications to Finite Field Mappings
proposals.motivation: The theory of exceptional polynomials over finite fields, where a polynomial induces a permutation for infinitely many extensions, is a cornerstone of finite field arithmetic with deep ties to the Carlitz-Wan conjecture and the construction of permutation polynomials. However, this theory has been developed almost exclusively for separable polynomials. The systematic study of inseparable exceptional polynomials and their associated non-separable extensions remains a significant gap, limiting our understanding of the full landscape of exceptional mappings and their potential applications in cryptography and coding theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robust, non-trivial theory of inseparable exceptional polynomials exists, leading to new infinite families of permutation mappings on finite fields and providing a novel perspective on the structure of exceptional extensions in positive characteristic.
proposals.method: We will define a suitable notion of exceptionality for inseparable polynomials, requiring the induced map on rational points to be bijective for infinitely many finite extensions L/K where the polynomial remains inseparable. The core technical approach involves analyzing the Galois groups of the polynomial's splitting field over the global function field K(t), adapting the Guralnick-Thompson classification strategy to the inseparable case. We will construct explicit families using techniques of composition and manipulation of linearized polynomials (p-polynomials) and their fractional counterparts, leveraging recent work on complete mappings of specific forms over F_{q^2} to guide our constructions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the foundational framework for inseparable exceptionality, classify initial families of such polynomials, and derive new infinite classes of permutation polynomials over finite fields. It will extend the scope of the Carlitz-Wan conjecture's conceptual framework to non-separable settings, potentially revealing new connections to arithmetic dynamics in positive characteristic.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the focus from the well-studied separable exceptional polynomials to their inseparable counterparts, a direction largely unexplored despite the natural prevalence of inseparability in positive characteristic. This requires developing new criteria for exceptionality that are not reliant on the standard separable Galois theory tools.

proposals.title: Quantifying Exceptionality: Analytic Densities of Permutation and Birational Maps on Algebraic Varieties
proposals.motivation: The study of exceptional functions, like permutation polynomials over finite fields, is a classical topic with applications in cryptography and coding theory. Recent work by the researcher and others has focused on classifying specific forms, but a statistical understanding of how prevalent such exceptional behavior is within large families of rational maps is lacking. This project aims to bridge this gap by applying analytic number theory to geometric settings, providing a measure-theoretic perspective on exceptional geometric properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Within natural families of rational maps between algebraic varieties (e.g., morphisms of bounded degree on curves or surfaces), the subset of maps exhibiting exceptional properties—such as being permutation maps on rational points or birational isomorphisms—has a well-defined asymptotic density that can be bounded or computed using analytic methods.
proposals.method: We will parameterize families of rational maps using moduli spaces or explicit polynomial forms over finite fields and number fields. For each family, we will define an exceptionality criterion (e.g., surjectivity on F_q-points) and construct an arithmetic function counting maps up to a height bound that satisfy it. Using character sum techniques (like Weil bounds) and sieve methods (e.g., the large sieve over function fields), we will estimate this counting function. The analytic core will involve deriving asymptotic formulas or upper bounds for the density of exceptional maps within the family.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first general framework for quantifying the rarity or commonness of exceptional maps in arithmetic geometry, moving beyond classification to statistical description. It will yield concrete density theorems for families extending permutation polynomials and birational maps, providing new insights into the structure of mapping spaces from a probabilistic viewpoint.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of geometric classification problems with the quantitative machinery of analytic number theory and sieve methods represents a novel synthesis. While these fields have developed in parallel, their systematic combination to study densities of geometric properties in parameter spaces is largely unexplored.

proposals.title: Asymptotic Density of Exceptional Polynomials over Finite Fields
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials, which are permutation polynomials over infinitely many finite fields, are central to finite field theory and have applications in cryptography and coding theory. While their characterization is a classical topic, the fundamental question of how rare they are among all polynomials remains quantitatively unexplored. A rigorous asymptotic density measure would provide a new probabilistic perspective on this important class of functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the density of exceptional polynomials among all polynomials of a given degree d over finite fields is zero as d or the field size grows, and that their distribution is highly structured, concentrated within specific, well-defined algebraic families.
proposals.method: We will define natural density measures by counting exceptional polynomials of bounded degree over the sequence of fields 𝔽_{q^n} for fixed q and growing n, and over 𝔽_q for fixed n and growing degree d. The core technical approach involves combining the deep classification theorems for exceptional polynomials (e.g., via Galois theory and the classification of exceptional covers of the projective line) with combinatorial sieving methods and estimates for the number of polynomials in the parameterized families described by these classifications. Key tools include the analysis of associated curve genera and the application of the Chebotarev density theorem for function fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative bounds on the scarcity of exceptional polynomials, formalizing the intuition that they are 'exceptional' in a metric sense. It will establish a new framework for studying the statistical properties of polynomial mappings over finite fields and may reveal density differences between various subclasses, such as exceptional complete mappings or permutation pentanomials.
proposals.novelty: Prior work focuses on existence and classification; this proposal shifts the paradigm to quantification and asymptotic enumeration, a non-obvious step requiring the synthesis of deep classification results with analytic number theory in function fields.

proposals.title: Dynamical Systems Arising from Exceptional Polynomials over Local Fields: Iteration, Galois Groups, and Ramification
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials over finite fields are rare, characterized by inducing permutations over infinitely many extensions. Their study is central to the Carlitz-Wan conjecture and the construction of exceptional extensions of local fields. However, the dynamical systems generated by iterating such polynomials on the algebraic closure of a local field remain largely unexplored, despite their potential to reveal deep arithmetic-geometric properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the exceptionality of a polynomial imposes strong constraints on the dynamical system it generates, leading to predictable structures in periodic points, ramification behavior in associated iterated extensions, and the structure of associated arboreal Galois representations.
proposals.method: We will systematically study the iteration of known classes of exceptional polynomials (e.g., permutation pentanomials, complete mappings of specific forms) on the valuation ring of a local field. The approach involves: 1) Analyzing the reduction modulo the maximal ideal to understand periodic points on the residue field. 2) Applying Hensel's lemma and Newton polygon methods to lift periodic points and study their ramification in the tower of extensions defined by iterated preimages. 3) Computing and comparing the arboreal Galois representation associated with the iteration with the Galois group of the exceptional extension, using tools from the theory of profinite groups and rigid analytic geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new bridge between the arithmetic of exceptional polynomials and discrete dynamics over local fields. It will produce a classification of dynamical properties for several infinite families of exceptional polynomials and may yield new criteria for exceptionality based on dynamical invariants. The results will also provide concrete examples of arboreal Galois representations with exceptional structure.
proposals.novelty: While exceptional polynomials and polynomial dynamics are mature fields, their intersection is novel. The explicit connection of the dynamical properties of a polynomial (like its periodic orbit structure) to the arithmetic property of being exceptional (inducing Galois extensions with specific groups) is a new and non-obvious direction.

proposals.title: A Hierarchy of Constructive Complexity for Exceptional Extensions of Local Fields
proposals.motivation: The study of exceptional extensions of local fields, such as those verifying the Carlitz-Wan conjecture, focuses on extensions with prescribed Galois groups and ramification. However, the computational and constructive aspects of the generating elements are often overlooked. In algorithmic number theory and cryptographic applications, the complexity of the minimal polynomial directly impacts the efficiency and feasibility of computations. A systematic study of elements defined by 'pathological' polynomials is needed to understand the limits of effective computation in local fields.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a formal hierarchy based on the constructive complexity of an element's minimal polynomial (e.g., coefficient size, approximability, definability) correlates with distinct arithmetic properties of the generated local field extension, and that such 'pathological' elements can be systematically constructed at each level of the hierarchy.
proposals.method: We will first define a hierarchy (e.g., Primitive Recursive, Computable, Limit-Computable, Non-Constructive) for elements in algebraic closures of Q_p and F_q((t)). Using techniques from the theory of permutation polynomials and complete mappings over finite fields, we will construct explicit families of elements whose minimal polynomials exhibit prescribed pathologies, such as rapid coefficient growth or non-standard approximations. We will then analyze the resulting extensions, comparing invariants like ramification filtration, Galois module structure, and trace forms to those from extensions generated by 'tame' polynomials like sparse binomials or trinomials.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first taxonomy of local field extensions based on the constructive complexity of their generators, yielding explicit examples of extensions that are inherently difficult to compute with. It will establish new links between constructive mathematics, the theory of exceptional polynomials, and local class field theory, offering practical lower bounds for computational number theory algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While exceptional polynomials and extensions are well-studied, classifying them by the computational pathology of their defining equations is a novel perspective. Bridging the gap between the abstract existence in the Carlitz-Wan theory and explicit, complexity-theoretic construction is an unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Asymptotic Counting of Exceptional Polynomials over Finite Fields via Analytic and Sieve Methods
proposals.motivation: Exceptional polynomials, which are permutation polynomials over infinitely many finite extensions of their base field, are central to finite field theory and have applications in cryptography and coding theory. However, their classification is incomplete, and no asymptotic results exist for their density among all polynomials of a given degree. Establishing such quantitative results is a fundamental open problem in the arithmetic of function fields.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the set of exceptional polynomials of a given degree d over 𝔽_q is sparse, and that analytic number theory techniques can establish an asymptotic upper bound of the form O(q^{αd}) for their count, where α < 1 is a constant depending on d.
proposals.method: We will adapt the large sieve for function fields, as developed by Kowalski and others, to the setting of rational functions. The core method involves parameterizing polynomials of degree d, applying character sum estimates over the Galois groups of the associated function field extensions, and using the sieve to bound the number of polynomials that are exceptional (i.e., whose compositional iterates remain irreducible). This will be combined with the classification of low-degree exceptional polynomials (e.g., Dickson polynomials, linearized polynomials) to handle base cases and refine bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first non-trivial asymptotic upper bounds for the number of exceptional polynomials, moving beyond qualitative classification. It will introduce a new sieve-theoretic framework for counting problems in polynomial dynamics over finite fields, potentially applicable to other questions about polynomial properties defined by factorization conditions.
proposals.novelty: While sieve methods are standard in analytic number theory, their systematic application to count exceptional polynomials—objects defined by a global dynamical property—is novel. This bridges Ding's expertise in explicit polynomial constructions with powerful analytic counting techniques not previously applied to this specific problem.

proposals.title: Characterizing Generating Polynomials for Exceptional Extensions of Local Fields
proposals.motivation: The Carlitz-Wan conjecture, proven by Cohen in the 1990s, established that extensions defined by exceptional polynomials are Galois with specific Galois groups. While the forward problem of determining when a polynomial defines an exceptional extension is well-studied, the inverse problem remains open. A systematic characterization of all polynomials that generate a given exceptional extension is crucial for understanding the structure of these extensions and for applications in coding theory and cryptography, where such polynomials are used to construct permutation polynomials over finite fields.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given exceptional, totally ramified extension L/K of local fields, the set of all polynomials f(x) in O_K[x] generating L/K (i.e., with L = K(α) and f(α)=0) can be explicitly described in terms of transformations of a single, canonical generating polynomial, governed by the action of the Galois group and specific power series compositions.
proposals.method: The approach will combine techniques from the theory of Lubin-Tate formal groups and explicit computation with polynomials. First, for a given extension, identify a canonical minimal polynomial using known classifications of exceptional polynomials (e.g., linearized binomials, Dickson polynomials). Then, analyze how other generating polynomials relate to this canonical one via transformations of the form f(φ(x)) or π * g(f(x)), where φ(x) is an invertible power series over the valuation ring and π is a uniformizer. The method will involve studying the ramification filtration and using Hensel's lemma to classify equivalences, building on recent work on complete mappings and permutation polynomials over F_{q^2}.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for the inverse problem in the theory of exceptional extensions, yielding a complete parametrization of generating polynomials. It will deepen the connection between local field theory and the combinatorial study of permutation polynomials, potentially leading to new constructions of complete mappings and permutation polynomials with prescribed differential uniformity.
proposals.novelty: While the forward direction (polynomial → extension) is classical, the inverse direction (extension → all polynomials) is unexplored. The novelty lies in applying the explicit computational methods from the study of permutation polynomials to solve a structural inverse problem in local Galois theory, linking two previously distinct threads in the researcher's recent work.

proposals.title: Exceptionality for Infinite Extensions: A Framework for the Carlitz-Wan Conjecture in the Algebraic Closure
proposals.motivation: The Carlitz-Wan conjecture, proven for finite extensions of local fields, characterizes exceptional polynomials via their associated Galois groups. Recent work, including Ding's on exceptional extensions, has deepened this finite-dimensional theory. However, the foundational question of whether the core notion of exceptionality—a polynomial inducing a permutation over infinitely many residue fields—remains meaningful and structurally constrained for infinite algebraic extensions is entirely unexplored, limiting our understanding of the asymptotic behavior of these objects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a coherent theory of exceptionality for infinite algebraic extensions can be developed, and that such 'infinitely exceptional' polynomials are severely restricted, potentially being compositions of linearized polynomials, monomials, and certain classic exceptional polynomials over the base field.
proposals.method: We will first define exceptionality for an infinite extension L/K as the property that a polynomial f in O_K[x] induces a permutation on the residue field of every finite subextension of L. The core technical approach involves an inverse limit construction over the directed system of finite subextensions, linking the property to the stability of the associated arithmetic monodromy group under the absolute Galois group. We will analyze specific forms from Ding's recent work, such as sparse multivariate polynomials and complete mappings, testing their behavior under infinite towers like the cyclotomic Z_l-extension or the full algebraic closure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first rigorous framework for studying permutation polynomials over infinite algebraic extensions, extending the Carlitz-Wan paradigm beyond the finite case. It will classify which known exceptional constructions retain their property in the limit, providing new insights into the interplay between polynomial dynamics and infinite Galois theory.
proposals.novelty: While exceptionality is well-studied for finite fields and their finite extensions, the conceptual leap to infinite algebraic extensions is novel. The method of using inverse limits and pro-finite Galois groups to tackle this question represents a significant and non-obvious synthesis of arithmetic dynamics and infinite Galois theory.

ideas.title: Model-Theoretic Classification of Low-Degree Exceptional Polynomials over Finite Fields
ideas.description: This research direction combines the exhaustive enumeration of low-degree polynomials from specific families over concrete finite fields with model-theoretic analysis. The explicit lists serve as a finite, computable ground truth against which to test conjectured first-order definitions for the property of being an exceptional polynomial (a permutation polynomial over infinitely many finite fields). By applying quantifier elimination and stability-theoretic tools to the structured data from the enumerations, one can aim to prove that the set of exceptional polynomials within a given family is definable in the language of rings, or to identify the minimal algebraic complexity required for its definition. This creates a feedback loop where concrete computation informs abstract model theory, and vice-versa, leading to new algorithms for identifying exceptional objects and deeper understanding of their logical structure.

ideas.title: Analytic characterization of bijection spectra for polynomial maps on projective spaces over finite fields
ideas.description: This research program seeks to characterize the set of dimensions n for which a given family of algebraic maps induces a bijection on the projective space P(F_q^n). It combines exact algebraic methods to derive necessary and sufficient bijection conditions with analytic number theory techniques to study the density and distribution of such n. The synergy lies in using the exact conditions to define an arithmetic function or indicator, whose summatory function and asymptotic behavior can then be analyzed using tools like Dirichlet series, sieve methods, or the theory of arithmetic progressions. This approach reveals whether bijective dimensions are prevalent, sparse, or distributed according to some deeper arithmetic law, providing a statistical structural theory for these algebraic dynamical systems.

ideas.title: Analytic and Probabilistic Study of Exceptional Maps on Varieties
ideas.description: This research program generalizes the study of exceptional functions (e.g., permutations on finite fields) to rational maps between algebraic varieties over finite fields or number fields. It applies the machinery of analytic number theory—such as sieve methods, character sums, and asymptotic density estimates—to investigate how often a given family of morphisms exhibits exceptional properties like being birational isomorphisms or having surjective/dynamically simple behavior on rational points. The synergy lies in quantifying the 'size' of exceptional loci within moduli spaces or parameter families, transforming existential geometric results into statistical theorems about the prevalence of complex geometric phenomena in arithmetic settings.

ideas.title: Construct local field extensions via elements with computationally pathological minimal polynomials
ideas.description: This research systematically constructs and classifies elements that generate exceptional finite extensions of local fields (like finite extensions of Q_p or F_q((t))) where the minimal polynomial is computationally pathological. Pathologies include polynomials with exorbitant coefficients, non-liftable p-adic approximations, or those definable only by an oracle or non-algorithmic process. The approach develops a formal hierarchy of 'constructive complexity' for generating elements, provides explicit examples at each level, and studies the arithmetic properties of the resulting extensions in contrast to those generated by 'well-behaved' polynomials.

ideas.title: Extending Algebraic Constructions to All Finite Fields via Galois Descent
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to generalize a mathematical construction from prime fields to all finite fields (prime powers). Drawing an analogy from crystallography, where complex structures are derived from simpler parent lattices via symmetry-breaking, we adapt this approach. In the mathematical context, the prime field F_p serves as the simple 'parent structure'. For the extension field F_{p^n}, we employ Galois theory: the field is an n-dimensional F_p-vector space with a cyclic Galois group generated by the Frobenius automorphism. The analogous solution is to take a construction naturally defined for F_p and reformulate it to be compatible (equivariant) with the action of this Galois group. This process, known as Galois descent or 'twisting', systematically extends the construction's validity from the base prime field to all its finite extensions, thereby achieving the desired generalization to all finite fields.

ideas.title: A Function Field Analogue of the Gross-Zagier Formula for Non-isotrivial Rank 1 Drinfeld Modules
ideas.description: This research systematically generalizes the Gross-Zagier formula—a pivotal result linking heights of Heegner points to derivatives of L-functions—from elliptic curves over number fields to rank 1 Drinfeld modules over global function fields. The core adversarial redesign addresses the fatal flaw of a vacuous generalization by targeting a major open problem: proving a Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer type formula in the non-isotrivial case. The work meticulously rebuilds the analytic machinery, replacing modular curves with Drinfeld modular curves, classical complex multiplication with CM theory for Hayes modules, and harmonic analysis on the upper half-plane with harmonic analysis on the Bruhat-Tits tree. The resulting formula provides a concrete, computable tool for constructing rational points and testing the BSD conjecture in positive characteristic.

ideas.title: Analytic density of exceptional Galois covers and their arithmetic monodromy groups
ideas.description: This research direction applies the quantitative, density-estimation methods of analytic number theory to the study of exceptional Galois covers of the projective line. The core objective is to define and estimate the natural density, within suitable parameter spaces (like the rationals or number fields of bounded discriminant), of points corresponding to covers that are exceptional (i.e., not generic in the sense of the Guralnick-Thompson conjecture). This provides a statistical measure of the rarity of these arithmetically special covers. By linking the analytic properties of these density functions to the classification of finite simple groups and the structure of arithmetic monodromy groups, the fusion yields novel constraints on which groups can appear with positive density, thereby connecting asymptotic enumeration to deep structural Galois theory.

ideas.title: Characterizing Bijections on Projective Spaces via Topological Invariants and Fixed-Point Analysis
ideas.description: This research idea expands the original goal of characterizing bijections on projective spaces P(F_q^n) by drawing an analogy from algebraic topology and dynamical systems. In those fields, determining if a continuous map is a homeomorphism often involves checking its action on algebraic invariants (like homology) and analyzing its fixed-point structure via the Lefschetz fixed-point theorem. Adapted to this finite geometric context, the project proposes to: 1) Define discrete algebraic invariants for functions on P(F_q^n), such as their induced action on the lattice of linear subspaces or on associated l-adic cohomology modules. 2) Develop a finite-field analog of a fixed-point theorem, where a necessary and sufficient condition for a constructed function to be a bijection is that its induced map on a chosen invariant module is an automorphism, and that the counts of fixed points for its iterates satisfy a specific combinatorial congruence condition derived from a Lefschetz-type formula over finite fields. This approach aims to translate topological solution methods into algebraic-combinatorial criteria applicable to functions over finite fields.

ideas.title: Exceptionality and Local-Global Principles for Polynomials over General Coefficient Rings
ideas.description: This research program systematically investigates whether a polynomial over a general coefficient ring (e.g., local rings, Dedekind domains, or rings of integers) that induces an exceptional extension of local fields must be induced by a base change from a classical exceptional polynomial over a finite field. It inverts the classical direction of implication while relaxing the coefficient field assumption. The core synergy lies in using the broader category of coefficient rings to construct novel counterexamples or prove general rigidity theorems, thereby determining the precise ring-theoretic properties that force a return to the classical finite-field models. This fusion connects arithmetic dynamics over rings to inverse Galois theory in a local setting.

ideas.title: Generalizing Exceptionality to Polynomials over Local Rings via Topological Methods
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that the study of exceptional polynomials (e.g., permutation polynomials) must be confined to coefficients in finite fields. Drawing an analogy from topological dynamical systems, where global behavior is understood through local linear approximations and perturbation theory, we propose a method to generalize exceptionality to polynomials with coefficients in more general rings, specifically local rings like the ring of p-adic integers Z_p. The analogous solution involves analyzing a system's stability by first examining its simpler tangent space dynamics. Adapted here, we would first characterize exceptionality for the polynomial reduced modulo the maximal ideal (i.e., over a finite field), then employ tools like Hensel's Lemma and p-adic analysis to 'lift' this property to the complete local ring, understanding the conditions under which exceptionality is preserved. This approach provides a principled pathway to relax the finite field coefficient assumption by embedding the problem in a richer topological-algebraic context.

ideas.title: Arithmetic Dynamics of Exceptional Loci on Varieties
ideas.description: This research direction applies the quantitative and Galois-theoretic tools of arithmetic dynamics to the problem of exceptionality for higher-dimensional algebraic varieties. Instead of merely classifying which subvarieties are preperiodic or have Zariski-dense orbits under a rational map, the goal is to study the iterative arithmetic complexity (e.g., canonical height, orbit uniformity, field of definition growth) of these exceptional loci themselves. This synergy enables a dynamical stratification of varieties based on the arithmetic behavior of their special subvarieties, leading to new conjectures and invariants that blend Mordell-Lang-type problems with dynamical equidistribution and unlikely intersection theory.

ideas.title: Model-Theoretic Definability and Classification of Exceptional Covers
ideas.description: This research program applies the model theory of pseudo-finite fields (infinite fields with the same first-order theory as ultraproducts of finite fields) to study exceptional Galois covers of the projective line. The core synergy is to treat families of exceptional covers, parametrized over finite fields, as definable objects in this stable theory, analyzing their definable sets and stability properties. This allows for a rigorous classification of which exceptional covers, or families thereof, are definable in the language of fields, potentially revealing new constraints and invariants. The approach aims to translate geometric and arithmetic exceptionality into model-theoretic properties like ranks and orthogonality to certain types, offering a new lens for the Grothendieck-Teichmüller program's investigation of these covers.

ideas.title: Global Exceptional Covers: Arithmetic and Geometric Constraints
ideas.description: This research program systematically studies Galois covers of the projective line over global fields (number fields and function fields) that are 'exceptional' in the sense that their Galois groups and ramification data are highly constrained and rare. The core synergy is to analyze these covers through the lens of local exceptionality: requiring that the cover, when completed at almost all places of the global field, yields an exceptional extension of the corresponding local field. This simultaneous local exceptionality across a dense set of places imposes powerful global arithmetic constraints, linking the cover's geometric invariants (genus, monodromy groups, ramification type) to the arithmetic of the base field. The goal is to classify the possible global exceptional covers, understand their moduli, and derive consequences for the inverse Galois problem and anabelian geometry in a global setting.

ideas.title: Inverse Mapping of Exceptional Structures via Crystallographic Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts an approach from crystallography where exceptional symmetry groups are analyzed to determine possible atomic arrangements, rather than assuming they must derive from known perfect lattices. In the mathematical context, we invert the traditional assumption: instead of asking whether an exceptional local field extension must come from a classical exceptional polynomial, we treat the extension's exceptional Galois group or ramification structure as the starting point. We systematically catalog all possible polynomial and non-polynomial constructions (including iterated compositions, rational functions, or even non-polynomial maps) that could produce such extensions, potentially discovering new exceptional structures that weren't previously recognized as such. This approach challenges the completeness of the classical exceptional polynomial classification by opening the possibility that exceptional extensions might have alternative, non-classical origins.

ideas.title: Arithmetic Dynamics of Specific Function Classes: Conjecture-Driven Analysis of Iteration
ideas.description: This research program rigorously applies arithmetic dynamics by first defining a constrained but fertile class of iterated functions, such as those commuting with endomorphisms of algebraic groups or those with prescribed reduction properties. The core is a specific, falsifiable conjecture about the arithmetic behavior of their orbits, like the uniform boundedness of preperiodic integer points or the structure of Zsigmondy sets for orbit points. The work then systematically employs relevant tools—including canonical heights, moduli spaces for dynamical systems, and p-adic analysis—to prove partial results, develop computational evidence, and refine the conjecture, ensuring a concrete and testable advancement.

ideas.title: From enumeration to explanation: Deriving constraints on low-degree realizability for families over finite fields
ideas.description: This redesigned approach begins by analyzing the algebraic structure of the family to establish theoretical necessary conditions on possible degrees (e.g., using the theory of Weil bounds, divisibility of polynomials, or properties of associated Galois groups). These constraints are then used to guide a targeted computational search, ensuring that all found examples are explained by the theory and that the search space is pruned effectively. The output is not just a list, but a complete classification for small degrees, accompanied by proofs of impossibility for degrees not observed, and structural explanations for those that exist. This transforms a blind enumeration into a theoretically grounded investigation that can inform generalizations to larger parameters.

ideas.title: Classifying functions via their monodromy groups using techniques from crystallographic symmetry classification
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the symmetry classification problem in crystallography to the original mathematical inverse problem. In crystallography, the finite enumeration of space groups solves the problem: 'Given the abstract structure of allowed symmetry operations (translations, rotations, etc.), classify all possible periodic patterns (crystals) that realize this symmetry.' Analogously, here we address: 'Given a bijective property described by a specific monodromy structure (a permutation group acting on sheets of a covering), classify all functions (or their meromorphic/analytic equivalence classes) that possess this monodromy.' The adapted methodology involves treating the monodromy permutations as generators of a discrete group action on a covering. By analyzing the combinatorial and topological constraints these permutations place on the gluing of sheets and the placement of branch points—much like analyzing how rotational axes must fit within a translation lattice—we aim to produce a complete classification or moduli space for functions with the given monodromy, potentially revealing a finite or parametrized list of possibilities depending on the genus and other invariants of the associated covering surface.

ideas.title: Adapting Renormalization Group Methods to Analyze Dynamical Stability of Exceptional Polynomials
ideas.description: This research adapts the Renormalization Group (RG) framework from statistical mechanics to investigate the dynamical properties of exceptional polynomials on local fields. The analogous solution involves coarse-graining a system to study parameter flows and fixed points that explain universal behavior. Here, we treat polynomial iteration as a renormalization transformation on the parameter space governing these polynomials and their associated field extensions. By analyzing the resulting RG flow, we aim to identify fixed points corresponding to polynomials with scale-invariant dynamical features and use the stability manifold around these points to systematically classify the long-term iterative behavior and the structure of the resulting extensions, linking dynamical stability to Galois-theoretic properties.

ideas.title: Definability and Complexity Bounds for Cryptographic Functions via Pseudo-Finite Dimensions
ideas.description: This research systematically studies the logical definability of polynomial-time computable functions over finite fields, with a focus on functions central to cryptography like the discrete exponentiation map. It uses the model theory of pseudo-finite fields to construct non-standard dimensions that reveal inherent definability constraints, linking these to circuit complexity lower bounds. The approach combines asymptotic analysis in finite fields with ultraproduct techniques to prove that certain functions are not definable in specific fixed-point logics, thereby providing a new lens on the hardness assumptions underlying cryptographic protocols. This yields robust, complexity-theoretic interpretations of model-theoretic properties, moving beyond pure abstraction.

ideas.title: Computational Dynamics of Exceptional Polynomial Monodromy on Local Fields
ideas.description: This research program computationally analyzes the monodromy groups arising from the iteration of exceptional polynomials over local fields, explicitly linking group-theoretic data to dynamical invariants like ramification portraits and Julia sets. By employing algorithms from computational group theory and number theory, we will systematically tabulate monodromy groups for parametrized families, searching for new correlations between group structure (e.g., primitivity, size) and dynamical behavior (e.g., wild ramification limits, stability). This synergy enables the discovery of new exceptional extensions through computational screening of dynamical systems, and conversely, uses the classified groups to predict the existence of polynomials with prescribed, complex dynamical properties.

ideas.title: Model-Theoretic Uniformity and Definability of Exceptional Extensions over Global Fields
ideas.description: This research program studies exceptional extensions of global fields (number fields and function fields) through the lens of the model theory of finite and pseudo-finite fields. The core synergy is to treat the collection of local completions of a global field as a class of structures whose ultraproducts yield pseudo-finite fields, allowing the application of Ax-Kochen/Ershov principles and definability theory. This approach aims to characterize when an extension of a global field is 'globally exceptional'—meaning its local behavior is exceptional at a set of places that is not explained by standard Galois-theoretic constraints—by analyzing the definability of the associated local extension maps in a uniform first-order language. The goal is to produce general transfer theorems that link the arithmetic of the global field to the definable sets in the ultraproducts of its local completions, offering new criteria for exceptionality that are intrinsic to the global structure.

ideas.title: Construct a Dynamical Moduli Space as an Invariant for Exceptional Extensions
ideas.description: This research constructs a specific dynamical system on a Berkovich analytic space associated to a moduli space of polynomials over a local field. The core aim is to prove that the exceptionality of a polynomial's associated field extension is equivalent to the presence of certain dynamical features—such as a superattracting periodic cycle or a critically finite structure—in this associated system. This provides a rigorous, functorial dictionary: arithmetic properties of the extension (e.g., ramification filtration) correspond to analytic properties of the dynamical system (e.g., geometry of Fatou components). The outcome is a unified classification theory where exceptional extensions are precisely those 'visible' as special points in this dynamical moduli space.

ideas.title: Adapting Rare Event Statistics to Estimate the Density of Exceptional Polynomials
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from statistical physics and rare event theory to quantify the density of exceptional polynomials. The analogous problem is estimating the probability of rare, high-energy states in a large physical system, which is solved using large deviation theory and biased sampling algorithms (e.g., Wang-Landau) to efficiently explore low-probability regions. The adapted idea applies this framework by designing a stochastic, biased exploration of the space of polynomials over a finite field (e.g., of degree n over F_q). By guiding a random process with a weight function that favors polynomials exhibiting 'exceptional' traits (like specific permutation or exceptionality properties), one can efficiently sample this rare subset. The observed visitation rates, when properly re-weighted, yield an estimate for the asymptotic density of exceptional polynomials as n and/or q grow, providing a computational-statistical solution to a problem where direct counting is infeasible.

ideas.title: A Systematic Study of Exceptional Failure of Weak Approximation Beyond the Brauer-Manin Obstruction
ideas.description: This research program provides a rigorous, measure-theoretic framework for generalizing the concept of exceptionality to higher-dimensional algebraic varieties. It focuses on smooth projective varieties over number fields and defines an exceptional variety as one where the failure of weak approximation is not accounted for by the Brauer-Manin obstruction—the primary known mechanism. The work will systematically survey families of varieties (e.g., Fano varieties, rationally connected varieties) by dimension, constructing examples and proving non-existence theorems to map the boundary of this phenomenon. The goal is to produce a classification theory that explains which geometric invariants control this deeper, 'exceptional' arithmetic behavior.

ideas.title: Inverse Classification of Functions via Non-Solvable Monodromy Groups
ideas.description: This research program aims to solve a refined inverse problem: given a specific non-solvable permutation or matrix group (like a large alternating group, a sporadic simple group, or an irreducible complex reflection group) as a target monodromy structure, classify all functions—particularly multi-valued transcendental functions—that realize this group as their monodromy group. It moves beyond cataloging existing groups to using the detailed theory of non-solvable groups (their representations, generating systems, and rigidity) as a set of blueprints. The synergy lies in using the constraints and properties of these groups to reverse-engineer and discover new differential equations or analytic continuations whose solutions have the prescribed monodromy, thereby linking abstract group classification to the concrete classification of special functions.

ideas.title: Monodromy Rigidity and Realizability: From Permutation Data to Analytic Constraints
ideas.description: This research program investigates the inverse problem of constructing functions from prescribed monodromy data, but with critical constraints to ensure depth. For a fixed function class (e.g., polynomials, rational functions, or solutions to specific ODEs) and a given permutation representation (monodromy group), it seeks to determine if a function in that class realizing the representation exists, and if so, to what extent it is unique. The core is to derive analytic consequences and necessary topological constraints (like genus, point configurations) directly from the group-theoretic input. This transforms the idea into a concrete bridge between discrete group theory and the analytic/topological theory of functions.

ideas.title: Definability and Structure of Exceptional Loci in Mixed Characteristic via Multi-Framework Model Theory
ideas.description: This research program rigorously analyzes the definability of 'exceptional sets'—such as torsion points of formal groups and special values of modular functions—in the context of valued fields of mixed characteristic. It employs a multi-framework model-theoretic approach, integrating tools from the model theory of the Witt Frobenius (for quantifier elimination and stability in positive characteristic) with p-adic Hodge theory (for deep arithmetic structure) and o-minimality over the reals (for the archimedean side). The core objective is to prove precise tameness results (e.g., finite-dimensionality, geometric triviality) for these definable sets, thereby establishing new transfer principles between characteristic zero and positive characteristic for Diophantine properties.

ideas.title: Characteristic-Aware Lifting and Decomposition for Finite Field Constructions
ideas.description: This adversarially improved framework systematically generalizes mathematical constructions from prime fields to all finite fields by directly addressing the structural rift between prime and extension fields. It introduces a two-stage process: first, a characteristic-aware lifting protocol that uses the trace map or a carefully chosen polynomial basis to embed the prime-field structure into the extension field while preserving essential algebraic properties. Second, a decomposition stage that, if needed, breaks the construction into components over a tower of subfields, ensuring the generalized object is both well-defined and retains the intended combinatorial or algebraic features. The approach is validated by an automated property-checking system that confirms the generalization does not inadvertently destroy the construction's core functionality.

ideas.title: Cataloging Low-Degree Polynomials via Systematic Enumeration
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from computational chemistry's problem of molecular conformer enumeration to the mathematical context of listing low-degree instances of a polynomial family for concrete q. In chemistry, one enumerates all valid, low-energy spatial conformations of a molecule by systematically exploring its rotational degrees of freedom within defined physical constraints. Analogously, for the target polynomial family, we will treat its defining parameters as degrees of freedom and the mathematical conditions (low degree, family-specific properties, fixed q) as constraints. The adapted solution is to design and implement a systematic enumeration algorithm—such as a bounded exhaustive search over a parameter space or a constructive generation following specific algebraic rules—to produce a complete catalog of all polynomial instances satisfying the 'low-degree' criterion. This concrete list serves as the foundational data for empirical pattern discovery, hypothesis generation, and testing conjectures about the family's structure in the SCALING_DUAL dimension.

ideas.title: Axiomatic Classification and Explicit Characterization of Exceptional Behaviors in Local Field Extensions
ideas.description: This research program first establishes a formal, hierarchical classification of what constitutes 'exceptional' behavior in local field extensions, based on a systematic study of deviations from generic Galois, ramification, and norm group structures. The core objective is then to develop explicit, checkable algebraic criteria for an extension to belong to each defined class within this taxonomy. The output will be a structured framework that not only provides criteria for known types of exceptionality but also maps the boundaries between different types and may reveal new, previously unstudied exceptional classes. This approach ensures the results are robust, foundational, and clarify the scope of any claimed 'explicit criterion'.

ideas.title: Hybrid analytic-computational characterization of bijection domains with effective exceptional bounds
ideas.description: Develop a three-part framework that first uses analytic number theory to establish the function is bijective for sufficiently large n with asymptotic density 1. Then apply effective sieve methods (like Brun's sieve or the large sieve) to obtain explicit bounds N such that the bijection property holds for all n > N. Finally, implement a complete computational verification for all n ≤ N using optimized algorithms, thereby achieving a complete characterization. This approach bridges asymptotic analysis with definitive finite verification, overcoming the inherent limitations of pure density estimates.

ideas.title: Constructing Exceptional Functions via Symmetry-Breaking Perturbations
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from quantum chemistry and molecular spectroscopy to the problem of finding large-degree exceptional functions with solvable monodromy groups. In spectroscopy, complex systems with large, non-solvable symmetry groups are made tractable by applying controlled perturbations that break symmetry down to a solvable subgroup, thereby simplifying analysis while retaining rich structure. Analogously, we propose to start with functions possessing large, exceptional (e.g., sporadic), and non-solvable monodromy groups. By designing and applying systematic algebraic or geometric perturbations—such as adding parameterized singularities, imposing specific local monodromy conditions, or restricting the function's domain—we aim to break the full monodromy group down to a maximal solvable subgroup. This symmetry-breaking approach provides a constructive method to generate new families of 'exceptional functions' of large degree whose monodromy is solvable, effectively challenging the focus on inherently non-solvable groups by building solvable versions from them.

The goal is to classify all global geometric or arithmetic objects (like function field extensions or l-adic sheaves) that realize a given set of local wild monodromy data (e.g., inertia groups, Swan conductors). This synthesizes the construction of families with bounded ramification with the monodromy-based classification of the inverse problem, aiming to determine the precise conditions under which a set of local wild ramification patches can be integrated into a globally consistent object.

ideas.description: This redesigned research program formulates specific conjectures about whether codes generated from the given algebraic functions can achieve known asymptotic bounds (like the Gilbert-Varshamov bound) or enable efficient decoding beyond standard limits. It employs a dual-track methodology: one track uses theoretical analysis to derive provable properties of the code families, while the other uses computational algebra and search algorithms to test the hypotheses against structured noise models. The outcome is a definitive classification of each function's coding-theoretic potential, measured against concrete benchmarks of optimality and complexity.

ideas.title: Apply Population Genetics Stability Analysis to Arithmetic Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the stability analysis framework from population genetics to arithmetic dynamics. In population genetics, the Hardy-Weinberg principle and related tools analyze how allele frequencies change—or remain stable—under iterative mating cycles, focusing on equilibrium states, stability conditions, and perturbation effects. By analogy, we can treat the iteration of a rational function or morphism over a number field or finite field as a generational process. The adapted research applies this perspective to identify and classify fixed points and periodic points (analogous to genetic equilibria), analyze their stability and basins of attraction under iteration, and investigate how small 'mutations'—such as variations in coefficients, reductions modulo primes, or approximations in local fields—propagate through the dynamical system. This approach aims to provide new criteria for stability, bifurcation, and long-term arithmetic behavior in dynamical systems, potentially revealing structures analogous to genetic drift or selection in number-theoretic contexts.

ideas.title: Adapting Topological Defect Classification to Inseparable Exceptional Extensions
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from condensed matter physics and topological field theory. The analogous problem is the classification and analysis of topological defects (e.g., vortices, monopoles) in ordered media, where exceptional defects arise under non-generic symmetry-breaking conditions and are studied using homotopy-invariant schemes. The solution involves identifying the relevant order parameter space, calculating its homotopy groups, and characterizing the stable defects. Adapted to the mathematical context, we propose to investigate inseparable field extensions by treating the splitting field of an inseparable polynomial as a constrained geometric or algebraic object. The goal is to develop a classification framework for 'exceptional' inseparable extensions—those that do not fit the separable paradigm—using invariants inspired by topological defect theory. This would involve creating a theory that categorizes extensions based on p-radical degree, differential modules, or non-standard Galois-like structures in characteristic p, thereby challenging the assumption that exceptional behavior is solely a feature of separable extensions.

ideas.title: Generalizing Exceptional Extensions via Statistical Physics Phase Transition Models
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to generalize the theory of exceptional extensions from local fields to global fields by drawing an analogy from statistical physics. In physics, local interaction rules on a lattice (analogous to exceptional extensions over completions of a global field) can give rise to a global phase transition, characterized by an order parameter and universality classes. The analogous solution uses the renormalization group to connect local and global descriptions. Adapted to number theory, the idea is to model the set of exceptional extensions across all places of a global field (number field or function field) as a system of local constraints. The research would seek to define a suitable global arithmetic invariant that acts as an order parameter, determining when a consistent global exceptional extension exists. The solution would involve developing analytic or adelic methods to integrate local data, potentially identifying new global analogues of the local conditions defining exceptionality, and classifying the global fields that admit such structures.
